4-1.q: How do fixed costs and variable costs change with changes in the cost driver?
o: Average variable cost increases as the cost driver increases
Total fixed cost may change if it is beyond the relevant range
Total variable cost does not change as the cost driver increases
Average fixed cost increases as the cost driver increases
4-1.e: This is a true statement. The relevant range is the range of activity within which the fixed cost remains unchanged. As long as the activity level of the cost driver remains within the relevant range, fixed costs do not change with changes in activity. However, if the activity level of the cost driver is either above or below the relevant range, the total fixed cost may increase or decrease.
wrongWhy: This is not an accurate statement. Total variable cost increases as the cost driver increases, but average variable cost does not increase as the cost driver increases. Variable cost per unit remains the same as the activity level of the cost driver changes. Average variable cost is basically the same as variable cost per unit, because average variable cost is total variable cost divided by the activity level of the cost driver.

This is not an accurate statement. Total variable cost does change as the cost driver increases. Total variable cost will increase as the cost driver increases and decrease as the cost driver decreases. Variable cost per unit does not change, and total variable cost is the variable cost per unit multiplied by the activity level of the cost driver; so as the activity level of the cost driver increases, total variable cost increases.

This is not an accurate statement. Average fixed cost is total fixed cost divided by the activity level of the cost driver. As long as the activity remains within the relevant range (the range of activity within which the fixed cost remains unchanged), fixed costs do not change with changes in activity. Average fixed cost decreases as the cost driver increases, because dividing an unchanged fixed cost amount in the numerator by an increased activity level in the denominator results in a lower average fixed cost. 2.

4-1.q: Cell Company has discovered that the cost of processing customer invoices is strictly variable within the relevant range. Which one of the following statements concerning the cost of processing customer invoices is incorrect?
o: The cost per unit for processing customer invoices will decline as the volume of customer invoices increases
The cost of processing the 100th customer invoice will be the same as the cost of processing the first customer invoice
The total cost of processing customer invoices will increase as the volume of customer invoices increases
The average cost per unit for processing a customer invoice will equal the incremental cost of processing one more customer invoice
4-1.e: This question is asking which of the four statements is not correct. Therefore, a statement that is not correct is the correct answer to the question. This statement is not correct, so it is the correct answer to the question. Since the cost of processing invoices is strictly variable, the cost per unit to process customer invoices will remain the same — not decline — as the volume of customer invoices increases.
wrongWhy: This question is asking which of the four statements is not correct. Therefore, any statement that is correct is a wrong answer. This statement is correct, so it is a wrong answer. Since the cost of processing invoices is strictly variable, the cost per unit to process customer invoices will remain the same as additional invoices are processed.

This question is asking which of the four statements is not correct. Therefore, any statement that is correct is a wrong answer. This statement is correct, so it is a wrong answer. Variable costs do increase in total as the volume of activity increases.

This question is asking which of the four statements is not correct. Therefore, any statement that is correct is a wrong answer. This statement is correct, so it is a wrong answer. Because the cost of processing invoices is strictly variable, the cost to process each invoice will be exactly the same. As a result, the incremental cost of processing one more customer invoice will be equal to the variable cost of processing one invoice. The average cost per unit for processing a customer invoice will also be equal to the variable cost of processing one invoice. Thus, the average cost of processing one more customer invoice will be equal to the incremental cost of processing one more customer invoice, and both will be equal to the variable cost to process one invoice. 3.

4-1.q: A company that manufactures furniture is establishing its budget for the upcoming year. All of the following items would appear in its overhead budget except for the
o: overtime paid to the workers who perform production scheduling
fringe benefits paid to the production supervisor
cost of glue used to secure the attachment of the legs to the tables
freight charges paid for the delivery of raw materials to the company
4-1.e: Freight charges for the delivery of raw materials to the company are transportation-in costs. Transportation-in costs are landing costs, and they are inventoried along with the inventory they are related to. They are a part of direct materials cost and thus are direct costs, not overhead costs.
wrongWhy: Overtime paid to the workers who perform production scheduling is an overhead cost and so would appear in the company's overhead budget.
The production supervisor's salary as well as his or her fringe (employee) benefits are overhead costs and thus would appear in the company's overhead budget.
The cost of glue used to secure the attachment of the legs to the tables is an overhead cost and so would appear in the company's overhead budget. 4.

4-1.q: Conversion costs are
o: The sum of direct labor costs and all factory overhead costs
The sum of raw materials costs and direct labor costs
Manufacturing costs incurred to produce units of output
All costs associated with manufacturing other than direct labor costs and raw material costs
4-1.e: By definition, conversion costs are the costs of direct labor and factory overhead. It is the cost of converting the raw materials to the finished goods.
wrongWhy: This is the definition of prime costs.
Manufacturing costs include direct materials, which are not included in conversion costs.
This is the definition of overhead, and conversion costs include more than just overhead. 5.

4-1.q: Gleason Co. has two products, a frozen dessert and ready-to-bake breakfast rolls, ready for introduction. However, plant capacity is limited, and only one product can be introduced at present. Therefore, Gleason has conducted a market study, at a cost of $26,000, to determine which product will be more profitable. The results of the study follow. Sales of Desserts Sales of Rolls at $1.80/unit at $1.20/unit Volume Probability Volume Probability 250,000 0.30 200,000 0.20 300,000 0.40 250,000 0.50 350,000 0.20 300,000 0.20 400,000 0.10 350,000 0.10 The costs associated with the two products have been estimated by Gleason's cost accounting department and are shown as follows. Dessert Rolls Ingredients per unit $ 0.40 $ 0.25 *Gleason treats production tooling as a current operating expense rather than capitalizing it as a fixed asset. The advertising expense estimated by Gleason for the introduction of the new products is an example of a(n)
4-1.e: Discretionary costs are costs that are not essential to operating the busines and can be deferred. Marketing is often considered discretionary, and advertising is a subset of marketing.
wrongWhy: A committed cost is a cost that the company has already agreed to and committed itself to, even if the delivery and invoicing of the product or service have not yet taken place. Advertising is not a committed cost.
Opportunity cost is the lost revenue of not selecting the next best alternative. This does not relate to the cost of advertising.
Conversion costs include labor and overhead cost. Advertising is not a conversion cost. 6.

4-1.q: Butteco has the following cost components for 100,000 units of product for the year. All costs are variable except for $100,000 of manufacturing overhead and $100,000 of selling and administrative expenses. The total costs to produce and sell 110,000 units are
4-1.e: The total cost formula is: y = F + Vx Where: y = Total costs F = Fixed costs V = Variable cost per unit x = Total production In order to calculate the total cost to produce and sell 110,000 units, we need to determine the total fixed cost and the variable cost per unit. The total fixed costs are $200,000 ($100,000 each of manufacturing and selling costs). The total variable costs for 100,000 units are $450,000 ($200,000 raw materials + 100,000 direct labor + $100,000 manufacturing overhead + $50,000 selling/administrative expense). $450,000 divided by 100,000 units equals a total variable cost per unit of $4.50. Therefore, the total cost to produce and sell 110,000 units is: y = $200,000 + ($4.50 × 110,000) y = $695,000
wrongWhy: $650,000 is the total cost at a production and sales level of 100,000 units.

Total cost of $715,000 to produce and sell 110,000 units results from treating the fixed cost as though it were variable. Fixed costs do not change in total as a result of changes in volume as long as the volume remains within the relevant range. Since the question does not state that 110,000 units is outside the relevant range, we must assume that it is within the relevant range, and therefore, the fixed cost for 110,000 units is the same as the fixed cost for 100,000 units.
$495,000 is the variable cost to produce and sell 110 units. However, total cost includes fixed costs, as well as variable costs. 7.

4-1.q: A company manufactures chemical cleaning products that are sold by the case. The variable manufacturing costs per case are shown below. Monthly fixed costs are as follows. Management projects that it will manufacture 13,500 cases next month. What will be the total manufacturing overhead budget for next month?
4-1.e: The cost function is: ŷ = a + bx Where: ŷ = Total predicted costs a = Fixed cost b = Variable cost per unit of the cost driver x = Activity level of the cost driver Fixed manufacturing overhead cost is the sum of supervisor salaries ($42,000), plant equipment depreciation ($31,000), and factory insurance ($19,000), which equals $92,000. The variable manufacturing overhead cost per unit of the cost driver, which is number of cases manufactured, includes the indirect factory wages ($3.50), indirect material ($1.75), and miscellaneous variable overhead ($1.25), for a total of $6.50. Therefore, the manufacturing overhead cost function is: ŷ = $92,000 + $6.50x, where x is the number of cases manufactured. Therefore, at a manufacturing level of 13,500 cases, the total manufacturing overhead budget will be: $92,000 + ($6.50 × 13,500) = $179,750
wrongWhy: This total for budgeted manufacturing overhead costs includes budgeted advertising expense as part of fixed manufacturing overhead costs. Advertising expense is an administrative expense, not a manufacturing expense, and it should not be included in the calculation of the total manufacturing overhead budget for a month in which 13,500 units are to be produced. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This total for budgeted manufacturing overhead costs includes budgeted direct wages and direct material costs as part of variable manufacturing overhead costs. Direct wages and direct material are not manufacturing overhead costs and they should not be included in the calculation of the total manufacturing overhead budget for a month in which 13,500 units are to be produced. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This total for budgeted manufacturing overhead costs includes budgeted advertising expense as part of fixed manufacturing overhead costs and budgeted direct wages and direct material costs as part of variable manufacturing overhead costs. Advertising expense is an administrative expense, not a manufacturing expense; and direct wages and direct material are not overhead costs. Those costs should not be included in the calculation of the total manufacturing overhead budget for a month in which 13,500 units are to be produced. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 8.

4-1.q: Taylor Corporation is determining the cost behavior of several items in order to budget for the upcoming year. Past trends have indicated the following dollars were spent at three different levels of output. Unit Levels 10,000 12,000 15,000 In establishing a budget for 14,000 units, Taylor should treat Costs A, B, and C, respectively, as
4-1.e: Cost A is a semi-variable cost, a type of mixed cost. It has both a fixed component and a variable component. There is a basic fixed amount that must be paid regardless of activity, even if there is no activity. And added to that fixed amount is an amount which varies with activity. We can determine this because the total cost changes as activity changes, but the per unit cost changes as well. At an activity level of 10,000, the total cost is $25,000 while the per unit cost is $2.50; at a level of 12,000, total cost changes to $29,000 while the per unit cost changes to $2.42; and at a level of 15,000, total cost changes to $35,000 while the per unit cost changes to $2.33. Cost B is a fixed cost, because it remains the same in total ($15,000) within a range of from 12,000 units to 15,000 units. The budgeted volume is 14,000 units, and that volume is within the range of 12,000 to 15,000 units. Therefore, within the relevant range, Cost B is a fixed cost. Cost C is a variable cost. At an activity level of 10,000, the cost per unit is $1.50. At activity levels of 12,000 and 15,000, the cost per unit remains $1.50. To answer this question correctly, look at the total cost for each cost and whether it changes or remains the same when the activity level changes; then divide the total cost by the activity level for each cost and look at whether that changes or remains the same as the activity level changes. If the cost remains the same in total when the activity level changes, it is a fixed cost. If it changes in total but remains the same on a per unit basis, it is a variable cost. If it both changes in total and on a per unit basis, it is a semi-variable cost. Costs B and C are not semi-variable. See correct answer for a full explanation.
wrongWhy: To answer this question correctly, look at the total cost for each cost and whether it changes or remains the same when the activity level changes; then divide the total cost by the activity level for each cost and look at whether that changes or remains the same as the activity level changes. If the cost remains the same in total when the activity level changes, it is a fixed cost. If it changes in total but remains the same on a per unit basis, it is a variable cost. If it both changes in total and on a per unit basis, it is a semi-variable cost. Cost A is not a variable cost, Cost B is not a semi-variable cost, and Cost C is not a semi- variable cost. See correct answer for a full explanation.

To answer this question correctly, look at the total cost for each cost and whether it changes or remains the same when the activity level changes; then divide the total cost by the activity level for each cost and look at whether that changes or remains the same as the activity level changes. If the cost remains the same in total when the activity level changes, it is a fixed cost. If it changes in total but remains the same on a per unit basis, it is a variable cost. If it both changes in total and on a per unit basis, it is a semi-variable cost. Cost A is not variable cost. See correct answer for a full explanation. 9.

4-1.q: Manchester Airlines is in the process of preparing a contribution margin income statement that will allow a detailed look at its variable costs and profitability of operations. Which one of the following cost combinations should be used to evaluate the variable cost per flight of the company’s Boston-Las Vegas flights?
o: Airplane depreciation, baggage handling, and airline marketing
Fuel, food service, and airport landing fees
Flight crew salary, fuel, and engine maintenance
Communication system operation, food service, and ramp personnel
4-1.e: Variable costs are those that are incurred only when a product is made or a service is delivered. The variable cost per flight for an airline includes costs that are incurred each time a plane flies. Fuel is expended, food service is needed for the passengers, and airport landing fees are incurred with each trip a plane makes. Therefore, these are all variable costs.
wrongWhy: Variable costs are those that are incurred only when a product is made or a service is delivered. The variable cost per flight for an airline includes costs that are incurred each time a plane flies. Airplane depreciation is a fixed cost that will be the same no matter how many trips the plane makes. Baggage handling costs may be partially fixed and partially variable, because baggage handling equipment is a fixed cost while baggage handling employees may be either a fixed or variable cost. Airline marketing is a fixed cost that will be the same regardless of how many trips are flown. Thus, some but not all of these items are considered variable costs per flight to an airline.

Variable costs are those that are incurred only when a product is made or a service is delivered. The variable cost per flight for an airline includes costs that are incurred each time a plane flies. The salary for the flight crew is the same regardless of how many flights they make. (The word "salary" implies the flight crew is "on salary," meaning they receive a fixed amount each pay period regardless of how many flights they make or hours they work. The word "wages," on the other hand, implies that they are paid hourly. If "wages" were used here, their wage payments would be a variable cost.) Fuel is a variable cost because fuel is expended each time a plane makes a flight. Engine maintenance is partly fixed and partly variable, because some maintenance needs to be done periodically whether the plane flies or not, and some maintenance needs to be done based on flying hours. Thus, some but not all of these items are considered variable costs per flight to an airline.

Variable costs are those that are incurred only when a product is made or a service is delivered. The variable cost per flight for an airline includes costs that are incurred each time a plane flies. Communication system operation is a fixed cost that will be the same no matter how many trips the plane makes. Food service is needed for the passengers on each flight, so that is a variable cost per flight. Ramp personnel may be a fixed cost (if the employees are on salary and are paid the same amount regardless of how many flights are made) or it may be a variable cost (if the employees are paid an hourly wage). However, regardless of whether ramp personnel are classified as a fixed expense or a variable expense, not all of these items are considered variable costs per flight. 10.

4-1.q: In a traditional manufacturing operation, direct costs would normally include
o: Machine repairs in an automobile factory
4-1.e: Direct costs are those that can be easily traced to the specific units of products. There are two main categories of direct costs: direct labor and direct materials. Direct labor costs are the costs of labor that may be directly traced to the production of a unit. Direct material costs are the materials that are directly put into the finished product. The costs included in the direct material cost are all of the costs associated with acquiring it - the item itself, shipping, insurance and taxes, among others. Common examples of direct materials are plastic, metal, tires and components. Thus, wood in a furniture factory is a direct cost.
wrongWhy: Commissions paid to sales personnel are a classic example of a period cost related to sales (non-manufacturing overhead). These costs are neither direct nor indirect production costs.
Machine repairs and maintenance in an automobile factory are a good example of factory overhead (indirect) costs.
Electricity in an electronics plant is a classic example of factory overhead (indirect) costs. 11.

4-1.q: A cost that always can be physically traced to a cost object is
4-1.e: Prime costs are direct materials and direct labor and they can always be traced to a specific cost object. A cost object is anything for which costs are accumulated for managerial purposes: a specific product, job, product line, a market or certain customers.
wrongWhy: Indirect costs are costs that are not able to be directly traced to a cost object. This is what makes them indirect costs.
Not all overhead costs can be traced to a cost object. For example, variable overhead needs to be applied to all of the units produced and is not able to be traced.
Conversion costs are direct labor and overhead. Overheads are not able to be traced to cost objects and must be applied to them. 12.

4-1.q: An accountant for a biotechnology company is working on a projection of next year’s maintenance expenditures for the company’s laboratory equipment used in research and development. By analyzing past maintenance expenditures, the accountant was able to determine that future maintenance expenditures can be expressed by the following simple regression equation: Y = $20,000 + ($50 x X). The variable X is the number of hours the laboratory equipment has been in service. For the previous year, the variable X was determined to be 4,500 hours. If the accountant is projecting that X will be 5,000 hours next year, how much should the accountant project for next year’s maintenance expenditures?
4-1.e: The cost function for total costs is y = F + Vx, where: y = Total costs F = Fixed costs V = Variable cost per unit x = Total activity, in this case, total number of hours the equipment has been in service When fixed cost is $20,000, variable cost per unit is $50, and x is 5,000 hours, total cost will be: y = $20,000 + ($50 × 5,000) y = $270,000
wrongWhy: This is not the amount the accountant should project for next year's maintenance expenditures based on the information given in the question. The cost function for total costs is y = F + Vx, where: y = Total costs F = Fixed costs V = Variable cost per unit x = Total activity, in this case, total number of hours the equipment has been in service Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is not the amount the accountant should project for next year's maintenance expenditures based on the information given in the question. $245,000 is the total cost according to the cost function when the equipment has been in service for 4,500 hours. However, next year the number of hours the equipment has been in service is projected to be 5,000 hours. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is not the amount the accountant should project for next year's maintenance expenditures based on the information given in the question. The cost function for total costs is y = F + Vx, where: y = Total costs F = Fixed costs V = Variable cost per unit x = Total activity, in this case, total number of hours the equipment has been in service Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 13.

4-1.q: Roberta Johnson is the manager of Sleep-Well Inn, one of a chain of motels located throughout the U.S. An example of an operating cost at Sleep-Well that is both direct and fixed is
o: Advertising for the Sleep-Well Inn chain
4-1.e: Johnson's salary is both a direct cost and a fixed cost. It is a direct cost because it can be traced directly to a specific cost object, the specific motel where Johnson serves as manager. It is a fixed cost because as a manager, Johnson is paid a salary of the same amount each salary period, regardless of the number of hours she works.
wrongWhy: Toilet tissue used at the motel is a direct cost, but it is not a fixed cost. It is a direct cost because it can be traced directly to a specific cost object, the specific motel where it is used. But it is a variable cost because the amount of toilet tissue used varies with the number of guests at the motel.

Advertising for the Sleep-Well Inn chain is a fixed cost, but it is not a direct cost. Advertising is fixed because it does not vary with the amount of activity, which for a motel chain is the number of guests. But since the advertising is for the entire Sleep-Well Inn chain, it cannot be traced directly to a specific cost object such as a specific motel, so it is an indirect cost.

Water used at the motel is a direct cost, but it is not a fixed cost. It is a direct cost because it can be traced directly to a specific cost object, the specific motel where it is used. But it is a variable cost because the amount of water used varies with the number of guests at the motel. 14.

4-1.q: Roberta Johnson is the manager of SleepWell Inn, one of a chain of motels located throughout the United States. An example of an operating cost at SleepWell that is semi- variable is
4-1.e: A semi-variable cost has both a fixed component and a variable component. There is a basic fixed amount that must be paid regardless of activity, even if there is no activity. And added to that fixed amount is an amount which varies with activity. Electricity for a motel is a good example of a semi-variable operating cost. Some basic amount of electricity is required to maintain the facilities, even if the motel has no guests. Each guest uses some amount of electricity, so the amount of electricity used will increase above the basic level by a certain amount for each additional guest. So electricity is a semi-variable expense, because it has both a fixed and a variable component.
wrongWhy: Postage for reservation confirmations is a variable expense. It is the same amount for each confirmation mailed.
The security guard's salary is a fixed expense. It is the same regardless of how many guests the motel has.
Advertising in the yellow pages of the local telephone book is a fixed expense because it is the same regardless of how many guests the motel has. 15.

4-1.q: Variable costs are all costs
o: Of manufacturing incurred to produce units of output
That fluctuate in total in response to small changes in the rate of utilization of capacity
That do not change in total for a given period and relevant range but become progressively smaller on a per unit basis as volume increases
That are associated with marketing, shipping, warehousing, and billing activities
4-1.e: Variable costs are costs that are incurred only if activity (production or sales) takes place. Therefore, the more units that are produced, the greater the total variable costs of production will be. And if production decreases, so will the total variable production costs.
wrongWhy: While many manufacturing costs are variable, not all of them are variable.
This is the definition of fixed costs as fixed costs do not change in total as production levels change.
These are selling and administration costs and while some of these costs may be variable, some of them will be fixed. 16.

4-1.q: Discretionary costs are
o: Governed mainly by past decisions that established the current levels of operating and organizational capacity and that only change slowly in response to small changes in capacity
Those management decides to incur in the current period to enable the company to achieve objectives other than the filling of orders placed by customers
Likely to respond to the amount of attention devoted to them by a specified manager
4-1.e: Discretionary costs are costs that management may choose to incur or not incur without a substantial impact on the business. Employee training is often given as an example of a discretionary cost. There is no direct correlation between the level of activity and discretionary costs.
wrongWhy: This is the definition of a committed cost.
This is the definition of a sunk cost.
This is the definition of a controllable cost. 17.

4-1.q: Conversion cost pricing
o: Places heavy emphasis on indirect costs and disregards consideration of direct costs
Places heavy emphasis on direct costs and disregards consideration of indirect costs
Places minimal emphasis on the cost of materials used in manufacturing a product
Could be used when the customer furnishes the material used in manufacturing a product
4-1.e: Conversion costs are the costs of converting the materials to the finished product. They include direct labor and manufacturing overhead (both fixed and variable). As such, in a situation in which the customer provides the materials, conversion cost pricing could be appropriate because conversion costs would be the only costs involved.
wrongWhy: Conversion costs include direct labor and manufacturing overhead. Since direct labor is a direct cost and is also a conversion cost, it is not correct to say that conversion cost pricing disregards consideration of direct costs, so this statement is not true.

Conversion costs include direct labor and manufacturing overhead. Since manufacturing overheads are indirect costs and also conversion costs, it is not correct to say that conversion cost pricing disregards consideration of indirect costs, so this statement is not true.
Conversion cost pricing does not use materials at all in the pricing because materials are not a conversion cost. 18.

4-1.q: An imputed cost is
o: A cost that does not entail any dollar outlay but is relevant to the decision- making process
The difference in total costs which results from selecting one alternative instead of another
A cost that cannot be avoided because it has already been incurred
A cost that continues to be incurred even though there is no activity
4-1.e: An imputed cost is one that does not need to be paid out in cash, but it still relevant for decision making.
wrongWhy: This is the definition of an opportunity cost.
This is the definition of a sunk cost.
This is the definition of a fixed cost. 19.

4-1.q: A fixed cost that would be considered a direct cost is
o: A cost accountant's salary when the cost object is a unit of product
Board of directors' fees when the cost object is the Marketing Department
The rental cost of a warehouse to store inventory when the cost object is the Purchasing Department
A production supervisor's salary when the cost object is the Production Department
4-1.e: The supervisor's salary would be a fixed cost and when looking at the production department, this is a direct cost since the cost can be associated with the cost object (the production department).
wrongWhy: A direct cost is a cost that can be specifically attributed to the cost object. The accountant is not a direct cost when the cost object is a unit of product.

A direct cost is a cost that can be specifically attributed to the cost object. The board of directors fees are not directly traceable to the Marketing Department so they are not a direct cost of the Marketing Department.

A direct cost is a cost that can be specifically attributed to the cost object. The rent for a warehouse is not directly traceable to the purchasing department because other departments (for example, the production departments) also have control over how much inventory is held. 20.

4-1.q: A company sells a single product and its sales staff earns a commission of $20 per unit. It costs $15 to ship each unit, and a $5 promotional catalog is sent with each item. The cost of goods sold is budgeted at $30 per unit. The company’s fixed manufacturing overhead is budgeted at $45,000 per month. The budget for executive salaries is $40,000 per month, and the base salaries for the sales staff is $22,000. Other monthly expenses include advertising of $15,000, office rent of $10,000, office equipment depreciation of $8,000, and $3,000 of other administrative expense. If the company budgets its November sales at 5,000 units, what would be the total selling and administrative expense budget?
4-1.e: The cost function is: ŷ = a + bx Where: ŷ = Total predicted costs a = Fixed cost b = Variable cost per unit of the cost driver x = Activity level of the cost driver Total fixed sales and administrative expenses per month include: Executive salaries $40,000 Sales staff base salaries 22,000 Advertising expense 15,000 Office rent expense 10,000 Office equipment depreciation 8,000 Other administrative expense 3,000 Total fixed S & A expense $98,000 Total variable sales and administrative expenses per unit sold include: Shipping-out expense $15 Promotional catalog 5 Sales commissions 20 Total variable S & A expense $40 Total budgeted selling and administrative expense for a month in which sales, the cost driver for variable costs, are budgeted at 5,000 units is: $98,000 + ($40 × 5,000) = $298,000
wrongWhy: This answer results from including fixed manufacturing overhead cost of $45,000 as a fixed selling and administrative expense and including cost of goods sold of $30 per unit as a variable selling and administrative expense. Fixed manufacturing overhead is not a fixed selling or administrative expense, and cost of goods sold is not a variable selling or administrative expense. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from including fixed manufacturing overhead cost of $45,000 as a fixed selling and administrative expense. Fixed manufacturing overhead is not a fixed selling or administrative expense. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from including cost of goods sold of $30 per unit as a variable selling and administrative expense. Cost of goods sold is not a variable selling or administrative expense. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 21.

4-1.q: Within the relevant range, fixed cost per unit will
o: remain the same as the activity level decreases
decrease as the activity level increases
increase as the activity level increases
decrease as the activity level decreases
4-1.e: The "relevant range" is the range of activity over which a certain cost behavior holds true. The term is used most often to refer to fixed costs. Fixed costs do not vary in response to changes in activity as long as the activity level remains within a certain range. If the activity level drops below or rises above that range of activity, the fixed cost can change in total. An example of this effect is depreciation on factory equipment. As long as production does not rise beyond a certain level, the company will be able to continue production with its existing equipment. But if production requirements rise beyond the level that current equipment can meet, additional equipment will be required and depreciation will increase. As long as activity remains within the relevant range, fixed cost in total will remain the same. As the activity level increases, that same total fixed cost will be spread over a greater number of units, so the fixed cost per unit will decrease as the activity level increases.
wrongWhy: As long as activity remains within the relevant range, fixed cost in total will remain the same. However, fixed cost per unit will not remain the same because the same amount of total fixed cost will be spread over a greater number of units.

As long as activity remains within the relevant range, fixed cost in total will remain the same. As the activity level increases, that same total fixed cost will be spread over a greater number of units. That will not result in an increase in the fixed cost per unit as the activity level increases.

As long as activity remains within the relevant range, fixed cost in total will remain the same. As the activity level decreases, that same total fixed cost will be spread over a fewer number of units. That will not result in a decrease in the fixed cost per unit as the activity level decreases. 22.

4-1.q: Which one of the following refers to a cost that remains the same as the volume of activity decreases within the relevant range?
4-1.e: The variable cost per unit remains the same as the volume of activity decreases within the relevant range. The total variable cost will decrease as the volume of activity decreases; but the per unit cost will remain the same.
wrongWhy: The average cost per unit will not remain the same as the volume of activity decreases within the relevant range. The average cost per unit is the total cost for all units produced divided by the number of units produced. The total cost for all units produced will consist of some fixed and some variable costs. The fixed cost will not change as the production decreases, so the per unit average fixed cost included in the average cost per unit will increase as production decreases. That will cause the average cost per unit to also increase.

The unit fixed cost will not remain the same as the volume of activity decreases within the relevant range. The total fixed cost will remain the same, but the per unit fixed cost will increase as the volume of activity decreases within the relevant range.
The total variable cost will decrease as the volume of activity decreases within the relevant range. 23.

4-1.q: Alex Company had the following inventories at the beginning and end of the month of January: January 1 January 31 The following additional manufacturing data were available for the month of January: Alex Company applies factory overhead at a rate of 60% of direct labor cost, and any overapplied or underapplied factory overhead is deferred until the end of the year, December 31. Alex Company's prime costs for January were:
4-1.e: The prime costs are direct materials used and direct labor used. Direct labor used was $300,000, but the cost of direct materials used needs to be calculated. Direct materials used is beginning direct materials inventory, plus purchases, plus transportation-in, minus purchase returns and allowances, minus ending direct materials Direct labor used $ 300,000 Direct materials $134,000+$189,000+$3,000−$1,000− 201,000 used $124,000 Total prime costs $501,000
wrongWhy: This is direct labor used plus direct materials purchased. The prime costs are direct materials used and direct labor used.

Prime costs are direct materials used and direct labor used. This is a partial calculation of direct materials used, but it does not take into account transportation-in (an increase to DM used) and returns (a decrease to DM used).
This is only the direct materials used and does not include direct labor used, which is also a prime cost. 24.

4-1.q: Which one of the following is correct regarding a relevant range?
o: The relevant range cannot be changed after being established
Total fixed costs will not change
Actual fixed costs usually fall outside the relevant range
Total variable costs will not change
4-1.e: Within the relevant range total fixed costs do not change.
wrongWhy: The relevant range may be changed as circumstances change.
Actual fixed costs should not differ much from budgeted, so actual fixed costs should always fall within the relevant range.
In the relevant range, total variable costs will increase as output increases and decrease as output decreases. 25.

4-1.q: Which of the following is the best example of a variable cost?
4-1.e: A variable cost is a cost that changes in total as the level of activity (either sales or production) changes. Direct materials is a very good example of a variable cost, because it changes with the level of production.
wrongWhy: A variable cost is a cost that changes in total as the level of activity (either sales or production) changes. The corporate president's salary will not be affected by the level of activity either of sales or of production.

A variable cost is a cost that changes in total as the level of activity (either sales or production) changes. Interest charges will not be affected by the level of activity either of sales or of production.

A variable cost is a cost that changes in total as the level of activity (either sales or production) changes. Property taxes will not be affected by the level of activity either of sales or of production. 26.

4-1.q: A company operates 10 offices. In the prior year, the total cost of operating the offices was $1,000,000 of which $140,000 consisted of fixed costs. All else remaining equal, what will be the budgeted costs if the company were to operate 12 offices?
4-1.e: In order to solve this question, we first need to separate the fixed and variable costs. We are told that when there were 10 offices the total cost was $1 million, and of that, $140,000 was fixed costs. This means that with 10 offices, variable costs were $860,000 ($1,000,000 − $140,000). Therefore, the variable cost per office is $86,000 ($860,000 ÷ 10). To calculate the cost of 12 offices, we need to multiply the $86,000 variable cost by 12 and then add in the fixed cost of $140,000, which will remain unchanged even though there are now 12 offices. The cost function is y = F + Vx, where y = Total Costs, F = Fixed Costs, V = Variable Cost per Office, and x = the total number of offices. y = $140,000 + ($86,000 × 12) y = $1,172,000
wrongWhy: This answer treats the fixed cost as though it were a variable cost. The fixed cost portion of the total cost will not increase if the number of offices increases. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer may result from dividing the total cost of $1,000,000 by 10 and then multiplying by 12; and then deducting $172,000 from the result, which is 2 times the variable cost per office of $86,000. Dividing the total cost by 10 and then multiplying by 12 treats the fixed cost as though it were a variable cost; and the total budgeted cost for 12 offices should be increased by the amount of the variable cost for two additional offices, not decreased by it. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This is the variable cost of operating 12 offices, but it ignores the fixed costs for the company. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 27.

4-1.q: The relevant range refers to the range of activity levels within which
4-1.e: The relevant range is the range of activity within which the cost is fixed. This means that within the relevant range, an increase in activity will not cause an increase in the total fixed cost, and a decrease in activity will not cause a decrease in the total fixed cost.
wrongWhy: The relevant range is not a range of activity within which costs fluctuate.
The relevant range is not a range of activity within which production varies.
The relevant range is not a range of activity within which relevant costs are incurred. 28.

4-1.q: The term prime costs refers to
o: The sum of raw materials costs and direct labor costs
All costs associated with manufacturing other than direct labor costs and raw materials costs
Costs that are predetermined and should be attained
The sum of direct labor costs and all factory overhead costs
4-1.e: Prime costs include direct materials and direct labor.
wrongWhy: This is the definition of overhead and overhead is not a prime cost.
This is the definition of a standard cost.
This is the definition of conversion costs. 29.

4-1.q: A company has observed the following costs at different production levels. Unit Production Level 25,000 50,000 75,000 What are the cost behaviors of Costs A, B, C, and D, respectively?
4-1.e: These are the correct classifications of each cost. Cost A is a mixed cost because the cost per unit decreases as the production level increases, but the decrease is not as much as it would be if the cost were a fixed cost. At 25,000 units, the cost per unit is $0.13, decreasing to $0.0967 per unit at 75,000 units. Thus, Cost A has both fixed and variable components. Cost B is a fixed cost because it remains the same, $7,500, regardless of the production level. Also, the cost per unit decreases by a fairly large amount, from $0.30 per unit at a production level of 25,000 units to $0.10 per unit at a production level of 75,000 units. But the primary way to determine that Cost B is a fixed cost is to note that it does not change with changing levels of production. Cost C is a variable cost because the cost per unit remains the same at every production level, $0.11 per unit. Cost D is a mixed cost because the cost per unit decreases as the production level increases, but the decrease is not as much as it would be if the cost were a fixed cost. At 25,000 units, the cost is $0.07 per unit, decreasing to $0.04 per unit at 75,000 units. Thus, Cost D has both fixed and variable components. Cost C is a variable cost because the cost per unit does not change as production levels change. Cost D is a mixed cost because the cost per unit decreases as the production level increases, but the decrease is not as much as it would be if the cost were a fixed cost. Thus, Cost D has both fixed and variable elements in it.
wrongWhy: Cost A is a mixed cost because the cost per unit decreases as the production level increases, but the decrease is not as much as it would be if the cost were a fixed cost. At 25,000 units, the cost per unit is $0.13, decreasing to $0.0967 per unit at 75,000 units. Thus, Cost A has both fixed and variable components. Cost C is a variable cost because the cost per unit does not change as production levels change. Cost D is a mixed cost because the cost per unit decreases as the production level increases, but the decrease is not as much as it would be if the cost were a fixed cost. Thus, Cost D has both fixed and variable elements in it.

Cost A is a mixed cost because the cost per unit decreases as the production level increases, but the decrease is not as much as it would be if the cost were a fixed cost. At 25,000 units, the cost per unit is $0.13, decreasing to $0.0967 per unit at 75,000 units. Thus, Cost A has both fixed and variable components. 30.

4-1.q: A review of Plunkett Corporation's accounting records for last year disclosed the following selected information. Variable costs Direct materials used $ 56,000 Fixed costs In addition, the company suffered a $27,700 uninsured factory fire loss during the year. What were Plunkett's product costs and period costs for last year?
4-1.e: Product costs include: direct materials $56,000; direct labor $179,100; variable manufacturing overhead $154,000; and fixed manufacturing overhead $267,000, for a total of $656,100. Period costs include: variable selling costs $108,400; fixed selling costs $121,000; fixed administrative costs $235,900; and the uninsured factory fire loss of $27,700, for a total of $493,000.
wrongWhy: This answer results from classifying all variable costs as product costs and all fixed costs plus the uninsured fire loss as period costs. All variable costs are not product costs, and all fixed costs are not period costs.
This answer results from incorrectly classifying manufacturing overhead as period cost.

This answer results from incorrectly classifying the $27,700 uninsured factory fire loss as a product cost. Even though it is a factory cost, it should not be included in the product costs because it is not a usual cost of manufacturing. The cost of the product should not be increased because of this. 31.

4-1.q: Lar Company has found that its total electricity cost has both a fixed component and a variable component within the relevant range. The variable component seems to vary directly with the number of units produced. Which one of the following statements concerning Lar’s electricity cost is incorrect?
o: The variable electricity cost per unit of production will remain constant as production volume increases
The total electricity cost per unit of production will increase as production volume increases
The fixed electricity cost per unit of production will decline as production volume increases
The total electricity cost will increase as production volume increases
4-1.e: The total electricity cost per unit of production will decrease as production volume increases. The reason for this is in the fixed component of the electricity cost. As production increases, fixed costs remain the same within the relevant range. As a result, the same amount of fixed cost is being spread over a greater number of units as production increases. That means the fixed cost per unit produced will decline. The variable cost per unit will stay the same while the fixed cost per unit declines. Therefore, the total cost per unit will decline.
wrongWhy: This is a true statement. Variable costs per unit produced do not change as production volume changes.

This is a true statement. As production increases, fixed costs remain the same within the relevant range. As a result, as production increases, the same amount of the fixed cost component of the electricity will be spread over a greater number of units. That means the fixed component of the electricity cost per unit produced will decline.
This is a true statement. As production volume increases, total electricity cost will increase due to the variable component of total cost. 32.

4-1.q: Which one of the following items would not be considered a manufacturing cost?
o: Plant property taxes for an ice cream maker
Sales commissions for a car manufacturer
Cream for an ice cream maker
4-1.e: For a car manufacturer, any costs for things used to make cars are manufacturing costs. Sales commissions are not costs for things used to make cars. Therefore, they are not manufacturing costs.
wrongWhy: Ice cream is a product, and any costs for things used to make it are manufacturing costs. Property taxes on plant property are a cost that is necessary to provide the physical plant and plant equipment needed to manufacture the ice cream. Therefore, plant property taxes are manufacturing costs.
For an automobile manufacturer, any costs for things used to make automobiles are manufacturing costs. Cars must have tires. Therefore, tires for an automobile manufacturer are a manufacturing cost.
Ice cream is a product, and any costs for things used to make it are manufacturing costs. Cream is a manufacturing cost for an ice cream maker because it is used to make the ice cream. 33.

4-1.q: Many companies recognize three major categories of costs of manufacturing a product. These are direct materials, direct labor, and overhead. Which of the following is an overhead cost in the production of an automobile?
o: The cost of small tools used in mounting tires on each automobile
The delivery costs for the tires on each automobile
The cost of the laborers who place tires on each automobile
The cost of the tires on each automobile
4-1.e: The cost of small tools cannot be traced to an individual automobile so would be included in factory overhead.
wrongWhy: The delivery costs for each tire will be included in the cost of the tire and are therefore direct materials.
The laborers who place the tires on the automobile are direct labor and can be traced to each automobile.
The tires are direct materials and can be traced to each individual automobile. 34.

4-1.q: Hitchcock Industries has developed two new products but has only enough plant capacity to introduce one of these products this year. The company controller has gathered the following data to assist management in deciding which product should be selected for production. Hitchcock's fixed overhead includes proportional rent and utilities, machinery depreciation, and supervisory salaries. Selling and administrative expenses are not allocated to products. Cost per unit: Power Drill Power Saw Raw materials $ 22.00 $ 18.00 Total unit cost: $ 73.00 $ 44.00 Suggested selling price $ 88.98 $ 49.95 Actual research and development costs $180,000 $ 95,000 The total overhead cost of $13.50 for Hitchcock's power saw is a
4-1.e: Because there are both fixed and variable overhead costs, the total overhead cost is a mixed cost.
wrongWhy: A carrying cost is the cost of keeping inventory.

A committed cost is a cost for the company's infrastructure. Committed costs are costs that are required in order to establish and maintain the readiness to do business. Examples are fixed assets such as property, plant and equipment and intangible assets such as the purchase of a franchise. They are usually on the balance sheet as assets and become expenses in the form of amortization and depreciation.
A sunk cost is a cost that has already been spent and cannot be changed by any current or future decisions. 35.

4-1.q: Hitchcock Industries has developed two new products but has only enough plant capacity to introduce one of these products this year. The company controller has gathered the following data to assist management in deciding which product should be selected for production. Hitchcock's fixed overhead includes proportional rent and utilities, machinery depreciation, and supervisory salaries. Selling and administrative expenses are not allocated to products. Cost per unit: Power Drill Power Saw Raw materials $ 22.00 $ 18.00 Total unit cost: $ 73.00 $ 44.00 Suggested selling price $ 88.98 $ 49.95 Actual research and development costs $180,000 $ 95,000 The advertising and promotion costs for the product selected by Hitchcock will be
4-1.e: A discretionary cost is a cost that does not need to be incurred in the short-term and if it is not incurred, it will not cause great losses for the company. Advertising is a cost that does not need to be incurred and in the short-term not advertising will probably not have a great impact on sales.
wrongWhy: Opportunity costs are the lost benefits from the next best use of a resource. The opportunity cost of advertising would be the benefit that is given up by not using the money for something else.

A committed cost is a cost for the company's infrastructure. Committed costs are costs that are required in order to establish and maintain the readiness to do business. Examples are fixed assets such as property, plant and equipment and intangible assets such as the purchase of a franchise. They are usually on the balance sheet as assets and become expenses in the form of amortization and depreciation.
Mixed costs are costs that include both variable and fixed elements. We are not given enough information to determine if this is the case, but it appears that they are fixed costs. 36.

4-1.q: Hitchcock Industries has developed two new products but has only enough plant capacity to introduce one of these products this year. The company controller has gathered the following data to assist management in deciding which product should be selected for production. Hitchcock's fixed overhead includes proportional rent and utilities, machinery depreciation, and supervisory salaries. Selling and administrative expenses are not allocated to products. Cost per unit: Power Drill Power Saw Raw materials $ 22.00 $ 18.00 Total unit cost: $ 73.00 $ 44.00 Suggested selling price $ 88.98 $ 49.95 Actual research and development costs $180,000 $ 95,000 The costs included in Hitchcock's fixed overhead are
4-1.e: Because the costs for fixed overhead relate to items like rent, these are committed costs. A committed cost is a cost for the company's infrastructure. Committed costs are costs that are required in order to establish and maintain the readiness to do business. Examples are fixed assets such as property, plant and equipment and intangible assets such as the purchase of a franchise. They are usually on the balance sheet as assets and become expenses in the form of amortization and depreciation.
wrongWhy: Prime costs are the costs of direct material and direct labor.

Sunk costs are costs that have already been spent and cannot be changed by current or future decisions. Not all of the fixed overhead costs are sunk costs, though it is possible that some of them are.
Discretionary costs are costs that do not need to be incurred in the short-term. Since these are production costs, they do need to be incurred and are not discretionary costs. 37.

4-1.q: Parker Company pays each member of its sales staff a salary as well as a commission on each unit sold. For the coming year, Parker plans to increase all salaries by 5% and to keep unchanged the commission paid on each unit sold. Because of increased demand, Parker expects the volume of sales to increase by 10%. How will the total cost of sales salaries and commissions change for the coming year?
o: Increase by more than 5% but less than 10%
4-1.e: Total compensation includes salaries and commissions. The cost of salaries, the fixed component of total compensation, will increase by exactly 5%. The cost of commissions paid, the variable component, will increase by the level of activity, which is 10%. Because total compensation is a blend of these two costs, total compensation will increase by some amount between 5% and 10%.
wrongWhy: The salary part of compensation will rise by 5% and the commission portion will also increase because sales are expected to increase by 10%; so it is not possible for total compensation to rise by less than 5%.

The salary part of compensation will rise by 5% and the commission portion will also increase because sales are expected to increase by 10%; so it is not possible for total compensation to rise by less than 5%.
The salary part of compensation will rise by only 5%, so it is not possible for total compensation to rise by 10%. 38.

4-1.q: Fowler Co. provides the following summary of its total budgeted production costs at three production levels. Volume in Units 1,000 1,500 2,000 The cost behavior of each of the Costs A through D, respectively, is
4-1.e: Cost A is a variable cost. At an activity level of 1,000, the cost per unit is $1.42. At activity levels of 1,500 and 2,000, the cost per unit remains $1.42. Cost B is a semi-variable cost, a type of mixed cost. It has both a fixed component and a variable component. There is a basic fixed amount that must be paid regardless of activity, even if there is no activity. And added to that fixed amount is an amount which varies with activity. We can determine this because the total cost changes as activity changes, but the per unit cost changes as well. At an activity level of 1,000, the total cost is $1,550 while the per unit cost is $1.55; at a level of 1,500, total cost changes to $2,200 while the per unit cost changes to $1.47; and at a level of 2,000, total cost changes to $2,900 while the per unit cost changes to $1.45. Cost C is a fixed cost, because it remains the same in total ($1,000). Cost D is a variable cost. At an activity level of 1,000, the cost per unit is $1.63. At activity levels of 1,500 and 2,000, the cost per unit remains $1.63.
wrongWhy: To answer this question correctly, look at the total cost for each cost and whether it changes or remains the same when the activity level changes; then divide the total cost by the activity level for each cost and look at whether that changes or remains the same as the activity level changes. If the cost remains the same in total when the activity level changes, it is a fixed cost. If it changes in total but remains the same on a per unit basis, it is a variable cost. If it both changes in total and on a per unit basis, it is a semi-variable cost. Cost B is not a semi-variable cost. See correct answer for a full explanation.

To answer this question correctly, look at the total cost for each cost and whether it changes or remains the same when the activity level changes; then divide the total cost by the activity level for each cost and look at whether that changes or remains the same as the activity level changes. If the cost remains the same in total when the activity level changes, it is a fixed cost. If it changes in total but remains the same on a per unit basis, it is a variable cost. If it both changes in total and on a per unit basis, it is a semi-variable cost. Cost A is not a semi-variable cost and Cost B is not a variable cost. See correct answer for a full explanation.

To answer this question correctly, look at the total cost for each cost and whether it changes or remains the same when the activity level changes; then divide the total cost by the activity level for each cost and look at whether that changes or remains the same as the activity level changes. If the cost remains the same in total when the activity level changes, it is a fixed cost. If it changes in total but remains the same on a per unit basis, it is a variable cost. If it both changes in total and on a per unit basis, it is a semi-variable cost. Cost B is not a fixed cost. See correct answer for a full explanation. 39.

4-1.q: The marketing manager of Ames Company has learned the following about a new product that is being introduced by Ames. Sales of this product are planned at $100,000 for the first year. Sales commission expense is budgeted at 8% of sales plus the marketing manager's incentive budgeted at an additional 1/2%. The preparation of a product brochure will require 20 hours of marketing salaried staff time at an average rate of $100 per hour, and 10 hours, at $150 per hour, for an outside illustrator's effort. The variable marketing cost for this new product will be
4-1.e: Variable marketing costs are costs which are the same per unit sold and which vary in total with sales volume. The only variable marketing costs are the sales commissions and the marketing manager's incentive. Based on projected sales of $100,000, sales commissions are 0.08 × $100,000, or $8,000. The marketing manager's incentive is 0.005 × $100,000, or $500 based on the projected sales. Since both these costs will vary with sales volume, they are both variable costs. Total variable marketing costs for the new product are thus $8,500. The other costs are all fixed costs.
wrongWhy: This includes the sales commissions and the marketing manager's incentive expenses, which are both variable marketing costs since they are tied directly to sales. However, it also includes the cost of the marketing staff in producing the brochure. The cost to produce the brochure will not change based on the sales volume. Therefore, it is not a variable marketing cost.

This is the cost of the sales commissions, which are a variable marketing cost. However, the sales commissions are not the only variable marketing cost. The marketing manager's incentive is also tied to sales performance and will therefore vary with the sales volume.

This includes the cost of the sales commissions as well as the cost of the salaried marketing staff's time to produce the brochure. The cost of the brochure is not directly dependent on sales, making it a fixed cost. This calculation also does not take into consideration the marketing manager's incentive, which is tied to sales and so is a variable marketing cost. 40.

4-1.q: Committed costs are
o: Governed mainly by past decisions that established the current levels of operating and organizational capacity and that only change slowly in response to small changes in capacity
Those that fluctuate in total in response to small changes in the rate of use of capacity
Those management decides to incur in the current period to enable the company to achieve objectives other than the filling of orders placed by customers
Likely to respond to the amount of attention devoted to them by a specified manager
4-1.e: Committed costs are costs for the company's infrastructure. They are costs that are required in order to establish and maintain the readiness to do business. Examples are fixed assets such as property, plant and equipment and intangible assets such as the purchase of a franchise. They are usually on the balance sheet as assets and become expenses in the form of amortization and depreciation. Therefore, they are governed mainly by past decisions that established the current levels of operating and organizational capacity, and they change slowly in response to small changes in capacity.
wrongWhy: This is the definition of a variable cost.
This is the definition of a discretionary cost.
This is the definition of a controllable cost. 41.

4-1.q: All of the following are examples of imputed costs except
o: Assets that are considered obsolete that maintain a net book value
Lending funds to a supplier at a lower-than-market rate in exchange for receiving the supplier's products at a discount
The stated interest paid on a bank loan
4-1.e: An imputed cost is a cost that has to be inferred because it does not represent an actual payment made or expense booked. Stated interest paid on a bank loan would not be an example of an imputed cost, because it is a stated cost for which actual payment is made.
wrongWhy: Carrying obsolete assets on the balance sheet violates generally accepted accounting principles. Obsolete assets should be written off to expense, and thus continuing to carry them on the balance sheet results in an understatement of actual costs, which creates imputed costs, or costs that are not stated (the write-off expense not booked).

An imputed cost is a cost that has to be inferred because it does not represent an actual payment made or expense booked. An opportunity cost is one type of imputed cost. An opportunity cost is the amount a company could earn if it were use a resource for its next best alternative use. Lending money at a below-market interest rate is an imputed cost of purchasing the supplier's products.

An imputed cost is a cost that has to be inferred because it does not represent an actual payment made or expense booked. An opportunity cost is one type of imputed cost. An opportunity cost is the amount a company could earn if it were use a resource for its next best alternative use. Decelerated depreciation would be the opposite of accelerated depreciation and would result in understated depreciation expense. Therefore, decelerated depreciation would be an example of an imputed cost, because it understates actual costs, resulting in costs that are not stated. 42.

4-1.q: The estimated unit costs for a company using absorption (full) costing and planning to produce and sell at a level of 12,000 units per month are as follows. Cost Item Estimated Unit Cost Estimated prime costs per unit are:
4-1.e: Prime costs include direct materials and direct labor. In this question, the direct materials and direct labor are $32 and $20, respectively, for a prime cost per unit of $52.
wrongWhy: This answer incorrectly includes the variable manufacturing overhead.
This answer incorrectly includes the manufacturing and selling costs.
This answer incorrectly excludes the direct labor cost. 43.

4-1.q: The estimated unit costs for a company using absorption (full) costing and planning to produce and sell at a level of 12,000 units per month are as follows. Cost Item Estimated Unit Cost Estimated total variable costs per unit are:
4-1.e: Variable costs are direct materials ($32), direct labor ($20), variable manufacturing overhead ($15), and variable selling expense ($3), for estimated total variable costs of $70 per unit.
wrongWhy: Variable costs are direct materials, direct labor, variable manufacturing overhead, and variable selling expense. This answer incorrectly excludes the variable overhead and selling costs.

Variable costs are direct materials, direct labor, variable manufacturing overhead, and variable selling expense. This is direct labor, variable manufacturing overhead, and variable selling expense only.
Variable costs are direct materials, direct labor, variable manufacturing overhead, and variable selling expense. This is variable manufacturing overhead and variable selling cost only. 44.

4-1.q: The estimated unit costs for a company using absorption (full) costing and planning to produce and sell at a level of 12,000 units per month are as follows. Cost Item Estimated Unit Cost Estimated conversion costs per unit are:
4-1.e: Conversion costs include direct labor and manufacturing overhead, both variable and fixed. At a production and sales level of 12,000 units per month, the direct labor is $20 per unit and the manufacturing overhead is $21 per unit for a conversion cost of $41 per unit.
wrongWhy: Conversion costs include direct labor and manufacturing overhead, both variable and fixed. This is direct materials, direct labor, and variable manufacturing overhead.
This answer incorrectly excludes the fixed manufacturing overhead.
Conversion costs include direct labor and manufacturing overhead, both variable and fixed. This answer incorrectly includes the variable and fixed selling costs. 45.

4-1.q: The controller of an online retailer has negotiated a five-year contract with a shipping company to pay the following amounts annually for the delivery of its goods, regardless of the amount. Year 1 €1,000,000 What type of costs are these shipping expenditures?
4-1.e: The shipping costs are fixed costs because they do not change regardless of the volume of goods delivered.
wrongWhy: Mixed costs contain a fixed cost component and a variable cost component. A fixed cost stays the same in total regardless of activity, while a variable cost remains the same on a per-unit basis as activity increases or decreases, and so total variable cost increases as activity increases and decreases as activity decreases. Thus, a mixed cost will increase somewhat as activity increases and will decrease somewhat as activity decreases. The shipping costs are not mixed costs because they do not change regardless of the volume of goods delivered.

A variable cost remains the same on a per-unit basis as activity increases or decreases, and so total variable cost increases as activity increases and decreases as activity decreases. The shipping costs are not variable costs because they do not change regardless of the volume of goods delivered.

By-products are the low-value products that occur naturally in the process of producing higher value products, and by-product costs are the costs associated with the by-products. Shipping costs are not by- product costs. 46.

4-1.q: Contribution margin focuses on which one of the following?
o: The amount of revenue available to cover fixed costs
How competitive a company is compared to other companies
The total revenue expected during the year
4-1.e: The "amount of revenue available to cover fixed costs" is the revenue left after all variable costs have been deducted from it, which is the definition of contribution margin. It is the amount received from sales that is available to cover fixed costs or to generate operating income after the fixed costs have been covered. Thus, an entity's contribution margin is its total revenue less its total variable costs (including both manufacturing and non-manufacturing variable costs).
wrongWhy: Contribution margin does not focus on total variable manufacturing costs. An entity's contribution margin is its total revenue less its total variable costs (including both manufacturing and non-manufacturing variable costs).

Contribution margin does not focus on how competitive a company is compared to other companies. An entity's contribution margin is its total revenue less its total variable costs (including both manufacturing and non-manufacturing variable costs).

Contribution margin does not focus on the total revenue expected during the year. An entity's contribution margin is its total revenue less its total variable costs (including both manufacturing and non-manufacturing variable costs). 1.

4-2.q: Use of a standard cost system can include all of the following advantages except that it
o: allows employees to better understand what is expected of them
4-2.e: Standard costing prescribes expected performance and provides control, which is quantitative, not qualitative.
wrongWhy: This is an advantage of standard costing. Flexible budgeting and use of a standard costing system go together. One is not meaningful without the other.

This is an advantage of standard costing. Standard costing prescribes expected performance and provides control. The standards establish what the costs should be, who should be responsible for them, and what actual costs are under control. This can allow employees to better understand what is expected of them.

This is an advantage of standard costing. Standard costing prescribes expected performance and provides control. The standards establish what the costs should be, who should be responsible for them, and what actual costs are under control. This can assist in performance evaluation. 2.

4-2.q: Normal costing systems are said to offer a user several distinct benefits when compared with actual costing systems. Which one of the following is not a benefit associated with normal costing systems?
o: More timely costing of jobs and products
A more economical way of attaching overhead to a job or product
Improved accuracy of job and product costing
A smoothing of product costs throughout the period
4-2.e: The difference between normal and actual costing systems is in the way the amount of overhead to be applied to production is calculated. When normal costing is being used, a predetermined overhead rate is calculated (called an estimated normalized rate), using the expected overhead costs divided by the expected usage of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours). When the overhead is applied to production, this predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours) that was actually used to produce the product during the period. (Note: this is in contrast to standard costing, where the predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base allowed for the actual output.) When actual costing is being used, no predetermined overhead rate is calculated. Instead, at the end of each period (month, quarter, whatever), the total actual overhead costs incurred for the period are calculated and the total is divided by the total actual number of units produced to calculate the amount of overhead to be allocated to each unit produced. Since normal costing uses a predetermined overhead application rate and actual costing uses the actual overhead application rate, it is not true that normal costing provides improved accuracy of job and product costing. In fact, it is the other way around. Actual costing provides improved accuracy because it uses actual allocation rates.
wrongWhy: The difference between normal and actual costing systems is in the way the amount of overhead to be applied to production is calculated. When normal costing is being used, a predetermined overhead rate is calculated, using the expected overhead costs divided by the expected usage of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours). When the overhead is applied to production, this predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours) that was actually used to produce the product during the period. (Note: this is in contrast to standard costing, where the predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base allowed for the actual output.) When actual costing is being used, no predetermined overhead rate is calculated. Instead, at the end of each period (month, quarter, whatever), the total actual overhead costs incurred for the period are calculated and the total is divided by the total actual number of units produced to calculate the amount of overhead to be allocated to each unit produced. Since the totals of actual overhead costs incurred and actual units produced cannot be known until after the end of the period, normal costing has an advantage over actual costing in that it is more timely. Overhead can be allocated to products without having to wait for the end-of-period actual data on overhead costs incurred to become available.

The difference between normal and actual costing systems is in the way the amount of overhead to be applied to production is calculated. When normal costing is being used, a predetermined overhead rate is calculated (called an estimated normalized rate), using the expected overhead costs divided by the expected usage of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours). When the overhead is applied to production, this predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours) that was actually used to produce the product during the period. (Note: this is in contrast to standard costing, where the predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base allowed for the actual output.) When actual costing is being used, no predetermined overhead rate is calculated. Instead, at the end of each period (month, quarter, whatever), the total actual overhead costs incurred for the period are calculated and the total is divided by the total actual number of units produced to calculate the amount of overhead to be allocated to each unit produced. Since with actual costing a unique overhead application rate is calculated for each period based on the actual incurred overhead costs and the actual production volume for the period, actual costing requires more people hours to calculate the unique overhead rate for each reporting period. Normal costing is more economical because it uses a pre-set rate that is usually used all year.

The difference between normal and actual costing systems is in the way the amount of overhead to be applied to production is calculated. When normal costing is being used, a predetermined overhead rate is calculated (called an estimated normalized rate), using the expected overhead costs divided by the expected usage of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours). When the overhead is applied to production, this predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base (machine hours or direct labor hours) that was actually used to produce the product during the period. (Note: this is in contrast to standard costing, where the predetermined overhead rate is multiplied by the amount of the allocation base allowed for the actual output.) When actual costing is being used, no predetermined overhead rate is calculated. Instead, at the end of each period (month, quarter, whatever), the total actual overhead costs incurred for the period are calculated and the total is divided by the total actual number of units produced to calculate the amount of overhead to be allocated to each unit produced. Since overhead costs are not incurred smoothly during the year and production varies during the year, allocating overhead using actual costing will result in an amount of overhead being allocated to each unit that will vary each reporting period. Thus, normal costing has an advantage in that it smooths the product costs throughout the period by smoothing the amount of overhead applied to units produced each period. 3.

4-2.q: A company’s budget indicated it should produce 11,000 units of finished goods using 22,000 hours of direct labor at a cost of $20 per hour. Actual results showed that 10,000 units of finished goods were manufactured, utilizing 21,000 labor hours at $19.75 per hour. If the company used a standard cost system, the dollar amount of direct labor traced to the units manufactured would total
