As a result, the fixed manufacturing cost applied to the unsold units in inventory will be on the balance sheet instead of in Cost of Goods Sold on the income statement, and net income will be increased by the amount of fixed manufacturing cost attached to the unsold units in ending inventory. To increase net income by $30,000, the number of units in inventory will need to increase by $30,000 ÷ $20 fixed manufacturing cost applied per unit, or 1,500 units. The company's practice is to write off under- or over-applied manufacturing cost to COGS. If production is increased by 1,500 units, fixed manufacturing cost will be overapplied by 1,500 units × $20 per unit, or $30,000, assuming that actual fixed manufacturing cost is the same as budgeted fixed manufacturing cost. This overapplied fixed manufacturing cost will be cleared out at the end of the period with a credit to COGS of $30,000. So fixed manufacturing cost in COGS will be $30,000 lower than it would have been if the company had produced only the 5,000 units it planned to produce and that it was able to sell. Note that because the whole variance is written off (credited) to Cost of Goods Sold, Finished Goods Inventory will not be adjusted. That extra $30,000 will remain on the balance sheet in Inventory at year end, still attached to the 1,500 extra unsold units that were produced. Because of the overproduction which led to a variance and the resulting credit to Cost of Goods Sold expense, only $70,000 of the actual $100,000 of fixed manufacturing cost incurred will reach the income statement, whereas all $100,000 of it would reach the income statement if only 5,000 units were produced and sold. When in some future year the company cuts production in order to sell down the extra inventory it built up this year, each unit produced in that future year will bear more of that year's fixed overhead. Cost of goods sold for that future year will include the $30,000 from this year's fixed overhead (for the units sold out of inventory) plus the greater amount of fixed overhead per unit for the units sold from the future year's decreased production. As a result, cost of goods sold in that future year will be higher than it should be and net income will be lower than it should be. Thus the result of the overproduction in the current year is to move some of a future year's net income into the current year.
wrongWhy: This answer results from including fixed selling and administrative cost along with fixed manufacturing cost in the calculation. Only fixed manufacturing cost is relevant.

This answer results from assuming that all of the additional production will be sold. The problem does not say that the additional production will be sold. The expected sales are still only 5,000 units, and only the revenues and costs for units that are sold reach the income statement. Only the production increases, and that causes an increase to inventory on hand. Because the production increases, the amount of fixed overhead applied to production will be more than the actual incurred fixed overhead. That variance will be closed out by crediting the amount of the overapplication to cost of goods sold, which will increase gross profit and increase net income before taxes by the amount of the variance. However, this answer would result in an increase in net income before tax of only $4,920, which would not accomplish George's misguided goal.

This answer results from (1) assuming that current budgeted operating income is zero and operating income is to be increased by the full $50,000 of desired operating income instead of by $30,000; and (2) misreading the question as asking for the total number of units to be produced instead of the increase in the number of units to be produced. 5.

4-10.topic: Accounting for Overhead, Including Over- or Under- Applied OH
4-10.q: A review of the year-end accounting records of Elk Industries discloses the following information. Applied Manufacturing Pre-Closing Overhead Included Ending Account in Each Pre-Closing Balances Account Balance The company’s underapplied overhead equals $133,000, a material amount. On the basis of this information, Elk’s cost of goods sold is most appropriately reported as
4-10.e: The underapplied overhead should be prorated among work-in-process, finished goods, and cost of goods sold on the basis of the total amount of manufacturing overhead applied during production that is included in each pre-closing ending account balance (before the proration). Raw materials inventory is excluded from the proration because no manufacturing overhead costs are applied to raw materials inventory. Thus, the portion of the $133,000 in underapplied overhead that should be allocated to cost of goods sold is $98,000, calculated as follows: $1,120,000 / ($128,000 + $272,000 + $1,120,000) x $133,000 = $98,000 Since the overhead was underapplied, we should add this amount to the cost of goods sold pre- closing account balance to reach the appropriate amount in the cost of goods sold account: $2,545,000 + $98,000 = $2,643,000.
wrongWhy: This answer incorrectly allocates all of the underapplied overhead to cost of goods sold and also subtracts it from cost of goods sold. The underapplied overhead should be prorated among work-in-process, finished goods, and cost of goods sold on the basis of the total amount of manufacturing overhead applied during production that is included in each pre-closing ending account balance (before the proration). Furthermore, the portion of the underapplied overhead to be allocated to cost of goods sold should be added to cost of goods sold, not subtracted from it.

This answer incorrectly bases the proration of the underapplied manufacturing overhead on the pre- closing ending account balances, and it also incorrectly allocates some of the underapplied overhead to the raw materials. The underapplied overhead should be allocated to work-in-process, finished goods and cost of goods sold only. Raw materials inventory should be excluded from the proration because no manufacturing overhead costs are applied to raw materials inventory. Furthermore, the underapplied manufacturing overhead should be allocated on the basis of the total amount of manufacturing overhead applied during production that is included in each pre-closing ending account balance (before proration), not on the pre-closing ending account balances.

The answer incorrectly allocates all of the underapplied overhead to cost of goods sold. The underapplied overhead should be prorated among work-in-process, finished goods, and cost of goods sold on the basis of the total amount of manufacturing overhead applied during production that is included in each pre-closing ending account balance (before the proration). 6.

4-10.topic: Accounting for Overhead, Including Over- or Under-Applied OH
4-10.q: Alex Company had the following inventories at the beginning and end of the month of January: January 1 January 31 The following additional manufacturing data were available for the month of January: Alex Company applies factory overhead at a rate of 60% of direct labor cost, and any overapplied or underapplied factory overhead is deferred until the end of the year, December 31. Alex Company's balance in factory overhead control for January was:
4-10.e: When the actual costs were incurred, the factory overhead account was debited for $175,000. When the overhead was applied, the account was credited for $180,000 (60% of the $300,000 labor cost). This leaves a credit balance of $5,000 in the account and this represents an overapplied amount since the applied overhead was greater than the actual overhead.
wrongWhy: A credit balance in the overhead control account represents overapplied, not underapplied overhead.
The overhead was overapplied, which will give a credit balance in the overhead control account.
An overapplied overhead balance results in a credit balance in the overhead control account. 7.

4-10.topic: Accounting for Overhead, Including Over- or Under-Applied OH
4-10.q: Overhead costs usually include
4-10.e: When overtime must be worked, the premium (this is the amount that the wage increases for overtime work) that is paid to the workers for the overtime is usually considered to be factory overhead. However, if the need to work overtime is the result of a specific job or customer request, the premium should be charged to that specific job and not included in the overall overhead amount to allocate. Hence, in most cases overtime premiums are included in overhead costs.
wrongWhy: Abnormal spoilage is all spoilage in excess of the normal level of spoilage. The costs that have been allocated to the abnormal spoiled units will be expensed on the income statement in that period as a loss from abnormal spoilage. Thus, abnormal spoilage is not included in the overhead costs.
Materials price variances are usually closed to the cost of goods sold or to the cost of goods sold and work-in-process on pro-rata basis.
Prime costs are the costs of direct material and direct labor. These are the direct inputs and are not included in overhead costs. 1.

4-10.q: The Pohr is calculated as:
4-10.e: Pohr = Budgeted Manufacturing Overhead / Budgeted Activity Level. Both numerator and denominator use Budgeted amounts. This is calculated Before the period starts to allow timely product costing.

4-10.q: When overhead is Applied to production, the journal entry is:
4-10.e: Applying overhead: Dr. Work-in-Process / Cr. Manufacturing Overhead Applied. This moves applied overhead into the cost of production (Wip).

4-10.q: Under-applied overhead means:
4-10.e: Under-applied: Actual OH > Applied OH. Products were Undercosted (not enough overhead charged). Results in a Debit balance in the Moh account. At year-end, this increases COGS.

4-10.q: A large (material) under-applied overhead balance should be:
o: Prorated among Wip, Fg, and COGS
4-10.e: Large (material) variance should be Prorated among Wip, Fg, and COGS based on their relative ending balances. This allocates the variance proportionally to all inventory that absorbed overhead. Small amounts go entirely to COGS.

4-10.q: Pohr $20/Dlh. Actual Dlh 2,400. Actual OH $51,000. Under/Overapplied =
4-10.e: Applied = $20 x 2,400 = $48,000. Actual = $51,000. Variance = $51,000 - $48,000 = $3,000 Under-Applied (actual > applied).

4-10.q: The Fixed Overhead Volume Variance measures:
o: Budgeted fixed OH vs applied fixed OH
4-10.e: Fixed OH Volume Variance = Budgeted Fixed OH - Applied Fixed OH. Measures production volume vs the capacity level used in the Pohr. More production = favorable (more fixed OH absorbed per the rate).

4-10.q: Which account has a Credit balance when overhead is over-applied?
4-10.e: Moh is Credited when overhead is applied and Debited when actual overhead is incurred. Over-applied = applied > actual = more credits than debits = Credit balance in the Moh account.

4-10.q: Wip $40,000 (20%), Fg $60,000 (30%), COGS $100,000 (50%). Under-applied OH $10,000. Amount allocated to Fg =
4-10.e: Fg's share = 30% x $10,000 = $3,000. Under-applied means we need to Increase costs: Dr. Fg $3,000 / Cr. Moh $3,000 (along with other allocations to Wip and COGS).

4-10.q: Variable Overhead Efficiency Variance is driven by:
o: Labor hours used vs standard hours allowed
4-10.e: VOH Efficiency Variance = (Actual Hours - Standard Hours Allowed) x Standard Rate. It is driven by Labor Efficiency — same driver as the DL Efficiency Variance. If fewer hours are used than standard, the variance is favorable.

4-10.q: Actual overhead incurred is recorded by:
o: Dr. Moh Control / Cr. Various (Cash, A/P, Accum. Depreciation)
4-10.e: Actual overhead: Dr. Manufacturing Overhead Control / Cr. Various accounts (Cash, Accounts Payable, Accumulated Depreciation, etc.). Moh Control accumulates actual overhead on the Debit side.

4-10.q: The Pohr formula uses ___ figures for Both numerator and denominator:
4-10.e: Pohr = Budgeted Moh / Budgeted Activity Level. Both numerator And denominator use budgeted/estimated figures. This is calculated at the Start of the year before any actual costs are known.

4-10.q: Recording Actual overhead incurred, the account Debited is:
4-10.e: Dr. Moh Control / Cr. Various (Cash, A/P, Accumulated Depreciation). Moh Control is Debited for Actual overhead costs as they are incurred. It accumulates actual overhead on the debit side.

4-10.q: When overhead is Applied to Wip, the account Credited is:
4-10.e: Dr. Wip / Cr. Moh Applied. Moh Applied is Credited when overhead is applied to production. This represents the overhead allocated to products using Pohr x actual activity.

4-11.q: A resort company allocates IT expenses to its operating departments based on the number of computers in each operating department. The majority of the IT department’s expenses are payroll. About 80% of IT’s workload is to answer phone calls from operating departments for technical support while the remaining 20% of IT’s workload is to set up new computers. The Food and Beverage operating department complains that it is being allocated more than its fair share of IT expenses. This department has recently replaced most of its computers. Those new computers do not require the same high level of technical support required by old computers in other operating departments. The best solution to this problem is to
o: charge IT expenses to general and administrative expenses instead of allocating
establish a cost pool for computer setup and a cost pool for support and then allocate cost pools based on departmental usage
change the allocation base to the number of technical support phone calls answered
change the allocation base to time spent on answering technical support phone calls
4-11.e: Activity-based costing can provide the solution to this problem. Activity-based costing (ABC) is a method of allocating overhead costs to cost objects based on cost drivers, and it is not limited to allocation of manufacturing overhead. It can also be used for allocation of shared services and other costs. The cost drivers in this situation are phone calls from operating departments for technical support and setting up new computers. The company can establish a cost pool for computer setup and a cost pool for telephone support and then allocate the costs in the cost pools based on departmental usage. In activity-based costing, an activity cost pool is a group of indirect costs that are grouped together as an activity for allocation to cost objects based on a single cost allocation base. A cost object is anything that consumes an activity or activities. In this scenario, the cost objects are the user departments.
wrongWhy: Charging IT expenses to general and administrative expenses instead of allocating them is not s good solution, because the company would not be able to know how its IT services are being used by the user departments. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
Using number of technical support phone calls as an allocation base would not account for the time spent on setting up new computers. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Using time spent answering technical support phone calls as an allocation base would not account for the time spent on setting up new computers. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 2.

4-11.q: The use of activity-based costing normally results in
o: Decreased setup costs being charged to low-volume products
Equalizing setup costs for all product lines
Substantially lower unit costs for low-volume products than is reported by traditional product costing
Substantially greater unit costs for low-volume products than is reported by traditional product costing
4-11.e: Because of the fact that ABC usually uses more allocation bases than other methods, those low-volume units that may not use much of the overall machine hours or labor hours usually end up with more overhead costs allocated to them under ABC. Traditional costing systems use things like volume (number of units produced or sold) to allocate costs. Therefore, a low-volume product would not be assigned much of the cost to be allocated. Higher-volume products would get more of the costs under traditional costing. However, number of units may not be a good indication of how much that low-volume product actually costs to produce. For example, it takes just as much time to set up a machine for a production run of 10 units as it does for a production run of 100 units. So if activity-based costing is being used and cost per setup and number of setups per product are used to allocate costs, the cost per part produced to set up the machine for a low volume product will be higher than it would be for a high volume product, because the cost will be divided over a smaller number of units for the low-volume product. ABC uses multiple cost pools (one would be as setup costs) related to multiple individual drivers (such as number of setups per product for the setup costs pool). So there will usually be several such allocations done under ABC costing. When costs are allocated by means of that method, low-volume products will usually get more costs allocated to them than under traditional costing methods, and the costs allocated to the low-volume products will be more representative of their true cost.
wrongWhy: Under ABC there will probably be more setup costs charged to low volume products because the setup costs are probably allocated and distributed separately from other overhead costs.
Under ABC not all product lines will have the same setup costs because it is unlikely that every product line has the same number of setups required.
Usually, under ABC the items that are low in volume have more costs charged to them than under a traditional, or other system. 3.

4-11.q: New-Rage Cosmetics has used a traditional cost accounting system to apply quality control costs uniformly to all products at a rate of 14.5% of direct labor cost. Monthly direct labor cost for Satin Sheen makeup is $27,500. In an attempt to distribute quality control costs more equitably, New-Rage is considering activity-based costing. Quantity for Activity Cost Driver Cost Rates Satin Sheen Incoming material inspection Type of material $11.50 per type 12 types In-process inspection Number of units $0.14 per unit 17,500 units Product certification Per order $77 per order 25 orders The monthly quality control cost assigned to Satin Sheen makeup using activity-based costing (ABC) is:
o: $525.50 lower than the cost using the traditional system
$525.50 higher than the cost using the traditional system
4-11.e: Under the traditional system, overheads are allocated based on direct labor at a rate of 14.5% of direct labor. Direct labor costs were $27,500 and 14.5% of this is $3,987.50. Under ABC we will need to make three allocations, one for each activity. For incoming materials the amount charged to Satin Sheen is $138 (12 × $11.50). For in-process inspection it is $2,450 (17,500 × $0.14). For product certification it is $1,925 (25 × $77). Adding these three amounts together, we get $4,513, which is $525.50 more than what was allocated under the traditional system.
wrongWhy: The monthly quality control cost assigned to Satin Sheen makeup using activity-based costing will be higher than the amount using the traditional system, not lower.
This is the total of the three cost rates: $11.50 + $0.14 + $77.00. Since all of those cost rates are for different units, totaling them is meaningless.

This is the total of the amount that would be allocated using traditional costing and the amount that would be allocated using activity-based costing. The total of the two options has no meaning, since one or the other will be used but not both. 4.

4-11.q: Believing that its traditional cost system may be providing misleading information, an organization is considering an activity-based costing approach. It now employs a full cost system and has been applying its manufacturing overhead on the basis of machine hours. The organization plans on using 50,000 direct labor hours and 30,000 machine hours in the coming year. The following data show the manufacturing overhead that is budgeted. Budgeted Budgeted Activity Cost Driver Activity Cost Material handling No. of parts handled 6,000,000 $ 720,000 Cost, sales and production for one of the organization's products for the coming year are as follows: Prime Costs: Sales and Production Data: Expected sales 2,000,000 units Batch size 5,000 units Setups 2 per batch Total parts per finished unit 5 parts Machine hours required 80 MH per batch If the organization employs an activity based costing system, the cost per unit for the product described for the coming year would be:
4-11.e: Under the ABC method we will have $5.15 in direct materials and direct labor, which is the same as under the traditional costing method; but we will need to calculate the overhead on the basis of activity-based costing. In ABC, there are 4 calculations we will need to make as part of the overhead allocation. These are as follows per activity: 1. Material Handling – $0.12 per part ($720,000 ÷ 6,000,000) and there are 5 parts per unit. This is $0.60. 2. Setup Costs – $420 per setup ($315,000 ÷ 750). There are 2 setups per batch, for a cost of $840 for each batch of 5,000 units. This is $0.168 per unit ($840 ÷ 5,000). 3. Machining Costs – $18 per machine hour. There are 80 machine hours per batch, giving us $1,440 per batch of 5,000 units, or $0.288 per unit. 4. Quality Control – $450 per batch. This is $0.09 per unit ($450 ÷ 5,000). In total, these costs add up to $6.30 per unit.
wrongWhy: This answer assumes the incorrect number of machine hours.
This answer uses only one setup per batch instead of two.
This answer assumes that that there is only one setup for each batch and the machine hours were therefore only 80. 5.
data: Direct labor cost per unit = 0.05 DLH @ $15/DLH

4-11.q: Believing that its traditional cost system may be providing misleading information, an organization is considering an activity-based costing approach. It now employs a full cost system and has been applying its manufacturing overhead on the basis of machine hours. The organization plans on using 50,000 direct labor hours and 30,000 machine hours in the coming year. The following data show the manufacturing overhead that is budgeted. Budgeted Budgeted Activity Cost Driver Activity Cost Material handling No. of parts handled 6,000,000 $ 720,000 Cost, sales and production for one of the organization's products for the coming year are as follows: Prime Costs: Sales and Production Data: Expected sales 2,000,000 units Batch size 5,000 units Setups 2 per batch Total parts per finished unit 5 parts Machine hours required 80 MH per batch If the organization uses the traditional full cost system, the cost per unit for this product for the coming year would be:
4-11.e: The traditional system will include the costs for direct materials, direct labor and overhead. Direct materials and labor are $5.15 per unit so all we need to calculate is the overhead per unit. Under the traditional method, overhead is applied based on machine hours. The rate is $60 per machine hour ($1,800,000 budgeted costs ÷ 30,000 hours). Each unit requires 0.016 machine hours (80 machine hours in a batch ÷ 5,000 units in a batch). This means that each unit will have $0.96 of overhead applied ($60 per hour multiplied by 0.016 hours per unit). This gives a total cost of $6.11 per unit under the traditional method.
wrongWhy: An answer of $5.44 assumes that the overhead allocated to each unit produced is only $0.29, and that is based on the assumption that only $540,000 of machining costs is being allocated. Each unit requires 0.016 machine hours (80 machine hours in a batch ÷ 5,000 units in a batch). $540,000 machining costs ÷ 30,000 budgeted machine hours = $18 cost per MH. $18 × 0.016 MH/unit = $0.288 per unit. But under traditional costing, the total manufacturing overhead of $1,800,000 is allocated to production, so that is the amount that should be used to calculate the predetermined overhead allocation rate, not the machining costs only. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This answer assumes that 80 machine hours are required for the entire 20,000 units produced, not per batch. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This answer is based on the direct labor overhead rate. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 6.
data: Direct labor cost per unit = 0.05 DLH @ $15/DLH

4-11.q: The costing method that determines product cost by identifying the costs of individual tasks and then assigning these costs to products on the basis of the tasks needed to produce each product is known as
4-11.e: Activity-based costing determines product cost by identifying the costs of individual tasks and then assigning these costs to products on the basis of the tasks needed to produce each product.
wrongWhy: Job order costing does not determine product cost by identifying the costs of individual tasks and then assigning these costs to products on the basis of the tasks needed to produce each product. Job-order costing is a cost system in which all of the costs associated with a specific job or client are accumulated and charged to that job or client. Job-order costing can be used when all of the products or production runs are unique and identifiable from each other. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Process costing does not determine product cost by identifying the costs of individual tasks and then assigning these costs to products on the basis of the tasks needed to produce each product. Process costing is used to allocate costs to individual products when the products are all relatively similar and are mass-produced. Process costing is basically applicable to assembly lines and products that share a similar production process. In process costing, all of the costs incurred by a process are collected and then allocated to the individual goods that were produced, or worked on, during that period within that process. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Operations costing does not determine product cost by identifying the costs of individual tasks and then assigning these costs to products on the basis of the tasks needed to produce each product. Operation costing is a hybrid, or combination, of job-order costing and process costing. In operating costing, a company applies the basic operation of process costing to a production process that produces batches of items. The different batches all follow a similar process, but the direct materials input to each batch are different. (Operations costing is not tested on the CMA exam.) Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 7.

4-11.q: Which of the following statements about activity-based costing is not true?
o: In activity-based costing, cost drivers are what cause costs to be incurred
Activity-based costing is more likely to result in major differences from traditional costing systems if the firm manufactures only one product rather than multiple products
Activity-based costing is useful for allocating marketing and distribution costs
Activity-based costing differs from traditional costing systems in that products are not cross-subsidized
4-11.e: The ABC and traditional methods will be the same when only one product is produced because all overheads will be allocated to that one product, no matter what method is used. The more different products are produced, the more ABC and traditional costing will give different results.
wrongWhy: Cost drivers are what cause costs to be incurred.
ABC may be used to allocate marketing and distribution costs.

Cross-subsidization occurs when the costs are not properly allocated among products. This is more likely to occur under a traditional system than under ABC, because ABC typically uses many more allocations. 8.

4-11.q: The allocation of costs to particular cost objects allows a firm to analyze all of the following except
o: Why a particular product should be purchased rather than manufactured in- house
Whether a particular department should be expanded
Whether a product line should be discontinued
Why the sales of a particular product have increased
4-11.e: The allocation of costs to a cost object will not enable the company to analyze why sales of a particular product increased. This is because the sales of the item are connected to so many other factors: the price, the economy, and the actions of competitors, to name a few.
wrongWhy: The allocation of costs is a critical part of the make-or-buy decision that a company must make.
The allocation of costs will be part of the analysis of whether or not a department should be expanded. Without this allocation, management will not know if the department is profitable.
The allocation of costs will be part of the analysis of whether or not a product line should be discontinued. Without this allocation, management will not know if the product line is profitable. 9.

4-11.q: Which of the following would be a reasonable basis for allocating the material handling costs to the units produced in an activity-based costing system (ABC)?
o: Number of production runs per year
Amount of time required to produce one unit
Number of components per completed unit
Amount of overhead applied to each completed unit
4-11.e: There is a direct causal relationship between the number of components in a finished product and the amount of material handling costs incurred.
wrongWhy: This allocation basis is related to batch costs and not to individual unit costs.
This allocation basis is the traditional basis for allocating overhead costs to the units produced when the production process is labor-intensive.
This is not an allocation basis but rather the result of the allocation process when determining product costs. 10.

4-11.q: All of the following are likely to be used as a cost allocation base in activity-based costing except the
o: number of vendors supplying the materials used to manufacture the product
units of materials used to manufacture the product
number of different materials used to manufacture the product
cost of materials used to manufacture the product
4-11.e: Activity-based costing identifies the causal relationship between activities and the incurrence of cost, determines the underlying resource consumption cost drivers of activities, establishes cost pools related to individual resource consumption cost drivers, develops costing rates, and applies cost to product on the basis of activity consumption cost drivers. Cost allocations in ABC need to be based on activity consumption cost drivers, not on other costs. For example, a reasonable activity consumption cost driver for allocating receiving costs would be the number of purchased parts required for each product in proportion to all the purchased parts. If receiving costs were allocated according to direct material costs, then the product that had the most direct material costs would be allocated the most receiving costs. But that product would not necessarily contain the most purchased parts. It might contain only one small, very expensive purchased part. The receiving costs should be allocated among products on the basis of number of purchased direct material parts received for each product, not by the cost of the purchased parts received.
wrongWhy: In activity-based costing, the number of vendors supplying the materials used to manufacture the product may be used as a cost allocation base to allocate the sourcing and purchasing costs.
In activity-based costing, the units of materials used to manufacture the product may be used as a cost allocation base to allocate the receiving costs and material handling costs.

In activity-based costing, the number of different materials used to manufacture a product may be used as a cost allocation base to allocate the sourcing and purchasing costs as well as the receiving and material handling costs. 11.

4-11.q: Atmel Inc. manufactures and sells two products. Data with regard to these products are given below. Product A Product B Total budgeted machine hours are 100,000. The budgeted overhead costs are shown below. The cost driver for engineering costs is the number of production orders per product line. Using activity-based costing, the engineering cost per unit for Product B would be
4-11.e: Number of production orders is the activity consumption cost driver. Product B used 18 ÷ (12 + 18), or 60% of the total number of production orders. The total cost was $300,000, so 60% of that is $180,000, and $180,000 is the total engineering cost allocated to Product B. $180,000 ÷ 12,000 units of Product B produced and sold = $15 engineering cost per unit. This is not the correct answer. Please see the correct answer for an explanation. We have been unable to determine how to calculate this incorrect answer choice. If you have calculated it, please let us know how you did it so we can create a full explanation of why this answer choice is incorrect. Please send us an email at incorrect answer choice -- not its letter, because that can change with every study session created. The Question ID number appears at the top of the question. Thank you in advance for helping us to make your HOCK study materials better.
wrongWhy: This is the engineering cost per unit for Product A, not Product B. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This is the total engineering cost allocated to Product A divided by the number of units produced of Product B. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 12.

4-11.q: A company’s activity-based costs for the most recent period are as follows. Activity Budgeted Cost Engineering $ 250,000 The following data for Product S and Product T pertains to the most recent period. Product S Product T Using activity-based costing, the engineering cost assigned to Product S is
4-11.e: The total engineering hours for both products is 25,000 (10,000 for S and 15,000 for T), of which Product S uses 10,000 hours, or 40%. The total budgeted engineering cost is $250,000, and 40% of that, or $100,000, is assigned to Product S.
wrongWhy: This is one-third of the total budgeted engineering cost. Product S is not using one-third of the budgeted engineering hours, so Product S should not be assigned one-third of the budgeted engineering cost.
This is the amount of budgeted engineering cost assigned to Product T. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is one-half of the budgeted engineering cost. Product S is not using one-half of the budgeted engineering hours, so Product S should not be assigned one-half of the budgeted engineering cost. 13.

4-11.q: A company with three products classifies its costs as belonging to five functions: design, production, marketing, distribution, and customer services. For pricing purposes, all company costs are assigned to the three products. The direct costs of each of the five functions are traced directly to the three products. The indirect costs of each of the five business functions are collected into five separate cost pools and then assigned to the three products using appropriate allocation bases. The allocation base that would most likely be the best for allocating the indirect costs of the distribution function is
4-11.e: The number of shipments would be a good basis for the allocation of distribution costs because the more shipments that are made, the higher the distribution costs will be.
wrongWhy: The number of sales people would not be a good basis for the allocation of distribution costs.
The dollar sales volume would not be a good basis for the allocation of distribution costs because a very expensive item might be very small and have low distribution costs.
The number of phone calls would not be a good basis for the allocation of distribution costs. 14.

4-11.q: A manufacturing firm produces multiple families of products requiring various combinations of different types of parts. The manufacturer has identified various cost pools, one of which consists of materials handling costs. This cost pool includes the wages and employee benefits of the workers involved in receiving materials, inspecting materials, storing materials in inventory, and moving materials to the workstations; depreciation and maintenance of materials handling equipment (e.g., forklift trucks); and costs of supplies used as well as other related costs. Of the following, the most appropriate cost driver for assigning materials handling costs to the various products most likely is:
4-11.e: When we are trying to allocate the costs related to handling costs, the number of parts used in a particular item would be a very good allocation base. The more parts that are in a product, the more movement there will be related to that product.
wrongWhy: How many suppliers there are does not impact the costs of movement related to the different products produced.
Direct labor hours does not correlate to the costs incurred for materials handling.
The number of units produced does not correlate to costs incurred for materials handling. 15.

4-11.q: When using activity-based costing techniques, which one of the following departmental activities would be expected to use machine hours as a cost driver to allocate overhead costs to production?
4-11.e: Robotics uses machines to do the work of people. Machine hours (i.e., robot hours) would be an appropriate cost driver to allocate overhead costs to production.
wrongWhy: In a cafeteria, the work is labor-intensive, so machine hours would not be an appropriate cost driver to use to allocate overhead to production. Direct labor hours would be more appropriate.

Material handling is a labor-intensive job, so machine hours would not be an appropriate cost driver. Hours spent in material handling by employees charged with that responsibility would be appropriate to use for the allocation of overhead to production.

Machine setups are labor-intensive, requiring time from the plant supervisor and perhaps an engineer. Hours spent on setups by the plant supervisor and the engineer would be appropriate to use for the allocation of their salaries, which would be overhead, to production. 16.

4-11.q: Pelder Products Company manufactures two types of engineering diagnostic equipment used in construction. The two products are based upon different technologies, x-ray and ultra-sound, but are manufactured in the same factory. Pelder has computed the manufacturing cost of the x-ray and ultra-sound products by adding together direct materials, direct labor, and overhead cost applied based on the number of direct labor hours. The factory has three overhead departments that support the single production line that makes both products. Budgeted overhead spending for the departments is as follows. D E P A R T M E N T Engineering Material Design Handling Setup Total $6,000 $5,000 $3,000 $14,000 Pelder's budgeted manufacturing activities and costs for the period are as follows. P R O D U C T X-Ray Ultra-Sound The budgeted cost to manufacture one ultra-sound machine using the activity-based costing method is
4-11.e: The per unit costs for direct materials and labor are simply the total budgeted costs as given divided by the number of units budgeted to be produced. Direct materials cost per unit for the ultrasound machine is $8,000 ÷ 100, or $80 per unit. Direct labor cost per unit for the ultrasound machine is $12,000 ÷ 100, or $120 per unit. Using ABC to allocate the budgeted overhead, costs in each cost pool (engineering design, material handling, and setups) will be allocated to each product and then allocated to each unit of each product. Engineering design - number of engineering changes budgeted for each product: Total budgeted usage is X-ray 2 + Ultrasound 1, for a total budgeted usage of 3. The total budgeted cost of $6,000 is divided by 3 to calculate the cost per change of $2,000. Ultrasound is budgeted for one change, so the budgeted engineering design cost for the 100 units of ultrasound machines budgeted to be manufactured is $2,000. Since 100 units are budgeted to be manufactured, the budgeted engineering design cost per unit is $2,000 ÷ 100, or $20 per unit. Material handling - number of parts budgeted to be used for each product: Total budgeted usage is X-ray 400 + Ultrasound 600, or 1,000 parts. The total cost of $5,000 is divided by 1,000 to calculate the budgeted cost per part used of $5. Ultrasound is budgeted to use 600 parts to manufacture 100 units, or 6 parts per unit. The budgeted material handling cost per unit is $5 × 6 = $30. Setup costs - number of product setups budgeted for each product: Total budgeted usage is X-ray 8 + Ultrasound 7 = 15. The total cost of $3,000 ÷ 15 = $200 per setup. Ultrasound machines are budgeted to use 7 setups, so the total setup cost allocated to all of the ultrasound production is $200 × 7, or $1,400. Since 100 units are budgeted to be manufactured, the budgeted setup cost per unit is $1,400 ÷ 100, or $14 per unit. Therefore, the total budgeted cost for one ultrasound machine is: Direct materials $ 80 Direct labor 120 Overhead: Engineering design 20 Material handling 30 Setup 14 Total $264
wrongWhy: One way of calculating this answer does not include any per unit costs for direct materials or overhead, and the per unit cost for direct labor is calculated incorrectly by multiplying the 300 direct labor hours used for ultrasound machines by the 75% of total direct labor hours used for ultrasound machine manufacturing. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from allocating the total factory overhead on the basis of number of units produced. When ABC is used, each classification of overhead costs should be allocated based on its cost driver: engineering design costs should be allocated according to the number of engineering changes required for each product; material handling costs should be allocated according to the number of parts used for each product; and setup costs should be allocated according to the number of product setups required for each product. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is the cost per unit under traditional costing when budgeted direct labor hours used are the allocation base for total manufacturing overhead. The question asks for the cost per unit using activity-based costing, not traditional costing. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 17.

4-11.q: Activity-based costing (ABC) is increasingly more feasible because of technological advances that allow managers to obtain better and more timely information at relatively low cost. For this reason, a manufacturer is considering using bar-code identification for recording information on parts used by the manufacturer. A reason to use bar codes rather than other means of identification is to ensure that
o: The movement of parts is easily and quickly recorded
Vendors use the same identification methods
Vendors use the same part numbers
The movement of all parts is recorded
4-11.e: Through the use of bar codes the movement and location of a product may be tracked quickly and easily without human involvement.
wrongWhy: Just because a bar code system is used does not mean that vendors will use the same identification methods.
Just because a bar code system is used does not mean that vendors will use the same part numbers.
Just because the unit has a bar code on it does not mean that the bar code will be read and the movement of the unit tracked each time it is moved. 18.

4-11.q: A company is considering the implementation of an activity-based costing and management program. The company
o: would likely use fewer cost pools than it did under more traditional accounting methods
would probably find a lack of software in the marketplace to assist with the related recordkeeping
should focus on manufacturing activities and avoid implementation with service- type functions
would normally gain added insights into causes of cost
4-11.e: With ABC, cost allocations are based on activities performed and what those activities cost. Some nonmanufacturing costs that are necessary to produce the products are included with manufacturing costs in determining the cost of each product. As a result, companies who use ABC normally gain added insights into the causes of their costs.
wrongWhy: More cost pools are used with ABC than under more traditional costing methods.

There is no lack of software available to assist with recordkeeping related to activity- based software. A firm considering implementation of an activity-based costing and management program would have many software options to choose from.
Activity-based costing is very useful for allocation of costs to service functions. 19.

4-11.q: A profitable company with five departments uses plantwide overhead rates for its highly diversified operation. The firm is studying a change to either allocating overhead by using departmental rates or using activity-based costing (ABC). Which one of these two methods will likely result in the use of a greater number of cost allocation bases and more accurate costing results?
o: ABC will result in a greater number of allocation bases; and departmental allocation will result in more accurate costing results
Departmental allocation will result in a greater number of allocation bases; and departmental allocation will result in more accurate costing results
Departmental allocation will result in a greater number of allocation bases; and ABC will result in more accurate costing results
ABC will result in a greater number of allocation bases; and ABC will result in more accurate costing results
4-11.e: Activity-based costing requires the use of a greater number of allocation bases; and activity-based costing also results in more accurate costing results. Activity-based costing focuses on individual activities as the fundamental cost objects. In ABC, the premise is that the cost of a finished product should include the cost of direct materials, direct labor and overhead costs directly attributable to that product, and it should also include a portion of the administrative costs necessary to produce the product. With ABC, the cost allocations are not based on usage of resources. Instead, they are based on activities performed and what those activities cost. ABC is much more detailed than traditional costing, because it uses many more cost pools, and each cost pool has its own cost driver. But it results in a more accurate cost for products.
wrongWhy: Departmental allocation does not result in more accurate costing results than ABC does. Activity-based costing focuses on individual activities as the fundamental cost objects. In ABC, the premise is that the cost of a finished product should include the cost of direct materials, direct labor and overhead costs directly attributable to that product, and it should also include a portion of the administrative costs necessary to produce the product. With ABC, the cost allocations are not based on usage of resources. Instead, they are based on activities performed and what those activities cost. ABC is much more detailed than traditional costing, because it uses many more cost pools, and each cost pool has its own cost driver. But it results in a more accurate cost for products.

Departmental allocation does not result in a greater number of allocation bases; and departmental allocation does not result in more accurate costing results. Activity-based costing focuses on individual activities as the fundamental cost objects. In ABC, the premise is that the cost of a finished product should include the cost of direct materials, direct labor and overhead costs directly attributable to that product, and it should also include a portion of the administrative costs necessary to produce the product. With ABC, the cost allocations are not based on usage of resources. Instead, they are based on activities performed and what those activities cost. ABC is much more detailed than traditional costing, because it uses many more cost pools, and each cost pool has its own cost driver. But it results in a more accurate cost for products.

Departmental allocation does not result in a greater number of allocation bases. Activity- based costing focuses on individual activities as the fundamental cost objects. In ABC, the premise is that the cost of a finished product should include the cost of direct materials, direct labor and overhead costs directly attributable to that product, and it should also include a portion of the administrative costs necessary to produce the product. With ABC, the cost allocations are not based on usage of resources. Instead, they are based on activities performed and what those activities cost. ABC is much more detailed than traditional costing, because it uses many more cost pools, and each cost pool has its own cost driver. But it results in a more accurate cost for products. 20.

4-11.q: A company is implementing an activity-based budgeting system. Set-up overhead is allocated based on set-up hours and manufacturing overhead is allocated based on direct manufacturing labor hours. Budget information is listed in the table below. Cost driver information Product A Product B Set-up time per batch 1.75 hours 1.25 hours Direct manufacturing labor time per batch 1.00 hours 0.75 hours The company plans to produce 1,000 units of Product A and 750 units of Product B. The activity rates are $100 per set-up hour and $150 per direct manufacturing labor hour. What is the total budgeted overhead?
4-11.e: To calculate the total budgeted overhead that the company began with in developing the activity rates given, it is necessary to work backward. Product A: 1,000 units are planned and there are 50 units per batch, so 20 batches are planned. Set-up overhead cost per batch: 1.75 hours × $100 per hour = $175. Set-up overhead cost for 20 batches: $175 × 20 = $3,500. Manufacturing overhead cost per batch: 1.00 direct labor hour × $150 per DLH = $150 Manufacturing overhead cost for 20 batches: $150 × 20 = $3,000. Product B: 750 units are planned and there are 25 units per batch, so 30 batches are planned. Set-up overhead cost per batch: 1.25 hours × $100 per hour = $125. Set-up overhead cost for 30 batches: $125 × 30 = $3,750. Manufacturing overhead cost per batch: 0.75 direct labor hours × $150 per DLH = $112.50 Manufacturing overhead cost for 30 batches: $112.50 × 30 = $3,375. Total budgeted overhead: Product A set-up overhead $ 3,500 Product A manufacturing overhead 3,000 Product B set-up overhead 3,750 Product B manufacturing overhead 3,375 Total budgeted overhead $13,625
wrongWhy: This answer results from using 50 units per batch to calculate the setup overhead and manufacturing overhead costs allocated to Product B. There are 25 units in each batch of Product B, not 50. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from using the number of units given per batch as the number of batches to be manufactured of each of the products. The number of batches to be manufactured of each of the products must be calculated. It is the number of units planned for the product divided by the number of units per batch. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from using 1.75 hours (the set-up time for Product A) instead of Product B's set-up time of 1.25 hours in calculating the set-up overhead cost for Product B. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 21.

4-11.q: The Chocolate Baker specializes in chocolate baked goods. The firm has long assessed the profitability of a product line by comparing revenues to the cost of goods sold. However, Barry White, the firm’s new accountant, wants to use an activity-based costing system that takes into consideration the cost of the delivery person. Listed below are activity and cost information relating to two of Chocolate Baker’s major products. Muffins Cheesecake Delivery Activity Average length of delivery 10 minutes 15 minutes Using activity-based costing, which one of the following statements is correct?
o: The muffins have a higher profitability as a percentage of sales and, therefore, are more advantageous
4-11.e: The delivery cost for each product is: Muffins: Cost per hour of $20.00 ÷ 60 minutes × 10 minutes per delivery = cost per delivery of $3.33. For 150 deliveries, the total delivery cost is $500. Cheesecakes: Cost per hour of $20.00 ÷ 60 minutes × 15 minutes per delivery = cost per delivery of $5.00. For 85 deliveries, the total delivery cost is $425. When the delivery cost is included, the profitability information for the two products is as follows: Muffins Cheesecake Revenue $53,000 $46,000 Cost of goods sold 26,000 21,000 Delivery cost 500 425 Net income $26,500 $24,575 The muffins, with net income of $26,500, are more profitable than the cheesecakes, with net income of $24,575. $26,500 − $24,575 = $1,925.
wrongWhy: The cheesecakes have a higher profitability as a percentage of sales.
The cheesecakes do not generate more profit than the muffins.
This does not take into consideration the cost of the delivery for each product. 22.

4-11.q: Young Company is beginning operations, and is considering three alternative ways in which to allocate manufacturing overhead to individual units produced. Young can use a plantwide rate, departmental rates, or activity based costing. Young will produce many types of products in its single plant, and not all products will be processed through all departments. In which one of the following independent situations would reported net income for the first year be the same regardless of which overhead allocation method had been selected?
o: The sales mix does not vary from the mix that was budgeted
All manufacturing overhead is a fixed cost
All ending inventory balances are zero
All production costs approach those costs that were budgeted
4-11.e: This is a new company just beginning operations. That fact tells us that the beginning inventory was zero. If the ending inventory is also zero, as in this answer choice, that tells us that all of the units the company produced during its first year of operation were sold during that year. Under all the ways of allocating manufacturing overhead to units produced, there would have been some variance between the actual incurred overhead and overhead allocated to production as the year passed. However, at the end of the year, the variances between the actual amount incurred and the amount of costs applied to the units produced would have been resolved, either by closing them out to COGS only or by allocating them between COGS and Ending Inventory. If ending inventory was zero, though, all of the variances would have been closed out only to COGS, regardless of which method of closing out variances the company was using. Since all production for the year was sold, 100% of actual costs incurred during the year would have been in COGS, regardless of whether a plantwide rate, a departmental rate, or activity based costing had been used. The amounts applied under each of the methods would have been different. However, when the amounts applied and the adjustments made to the COGS account to close out the variances were combined, the total amount of overhead in COGS expense would have been the same under all the methods. Sometimes it is easier to see something like this when you use numbers. So let’s say that the actual overhead incurred by Young Company during the year was $1,000,000. And let’s say that overhead applied under the various methods would have been as follows: Plantwide rate, $993,000 Departmental rates, $995,000 Activity Based Costing, $997,000 Using a plantwide rate, with total actual overhead incurred of $1,000,000, $993,000 would have been applied during production and all of that cost would have flowed to COGS because all of the production was sold. The variance between actual incurred overhead of $1,000,000 and applied overhead of $993,000, or $7,000, would have been closed out by debiting COGS for $7,000. Total overhead in COGS reported on the income statement would have been equal to the actual overhead incurred, or $1,000,000. Using departmental rates, with total actual overhead incurred of $1,000,000, $995,000 would have been applied during production and all of that cost would have flowed to COGS because all of the production was sold. The variance between actual incurred overhead of $1,000,000 and applied overhead of $995,000, or $5,000, would have been closed out by debiting COGS for $5,000. Total overhead in COGS reported on the income statement would have been equal to the actual overhead incurred, or $1,000,000. Using Activity Based Costing, with total actual overhead incurred of $1,000,000, $997,000 would have been applied during production and all of that cost would have flowed to COGS because all of the production was sold. The variance between actual incurred overhead of $1,000,000 and applied overhead of $997,000, or $3,000, would have been closed out by debiting COGS for $3,000. Total overhead in COGS reported on the income statement would have been equal to the actual overhead incurred, or $1,000,000. We can say this for certain only because all of the production was sold during the year. If some of the units produced had remained in inventory, unsold at the end of the year, the three different ways of applying the overhead to production may have resulted in three different divisions between COGS and Ending Inventory of the $1,000,000 overhead incurred during the year.
wrongWhy: A sales mix that does not vary from the mix that was budgeted would not cause reported net income to be the same for the first year regardless of which overhead allocation method had been selected. See correct answer for a full explanation.

All manufacturing overhead being fixed would not cause reported net income to be the same for the first year regardless of which overhead allocation method had been selected. See correct answer for a full explanation.

Production costs that are close to those that were budgeted would not cause reported net income to be the same for the first year regardless of which overhead allocation method had been selected. See correct answer for a full explanation. 23.

4-11.q: An accounting system that collects financial and operating data on the basis of the underlying nature and extent of the cost drivers is
4-11.e: Activity-based costing is the system that uses cost drivers to allocate costs.
wrongWhy: Cycle-time is the amount of time that the customer must wait for their order after the order has been placed.
In direct costing, fixed overheads are expensed in the period that they are incurred and cost drivers are not used. This is the same method as variable costing.
In variable costing, fixed overheads are expensed in the period that they are incurred and cost drivers are not used. This is the same method as direct costing. 24.

4-11.q: Atmel Inc. manufactures and sells two products. Data with regard to these products are given below. Product A Product B Total budgeted machine hours are 100,000. The budgeted overhead costs are shown below. Using activity-based costing, the per unit overhead cost allocation of receiving costs for product A is
4-11.e: The amount of cost to be allocated is $450,000 in receiving costs. The activity with respect to receiving orders is 50 orders for Product A and 150 orders for Product B. Product A's orders represent 50/200 of the total number of orders, or 25%. 25% of the $450,000 cost is $112,500. 30,000 units of Product A were produced and sold, so the per unit allocation of receiving costs for Product A is $112,500 ÷ 30,000 units = $3.75/unit. This is the amount of receiving costs allocated to each unit of Product B. The problem asks for the per unit cost allocation of receiving costs for Product A.
wrongWhy: This answer results from allocating receiving costs, engineering costs, machine setup costs and inspection costs for Product A, using receiving orders per product line to allocate receiving costs, production orders per product line to allocate engineering costs, production runs to allocate machine setup costs, and number of inspections to allocate inspection costs. The problem asks only for the per unit cost allocation of receiving costs for Product A.

This answer results from dividing the total budgeted overhead cost ($975,000) by total budgeted machine hours (100,000) to calculate a predetermined total overhead rate per budgeted machine hour of $9.75. Then, the predetermined overhead rate per budgeted machine hour is multiplied by the 2 machine hours required per unit of Product A to calculate a total overhead cost of $19.50 per unit of Product A. There are two problems with this approach: (1) This approach describes traditional overhead allocation using standard costing and allocating the overhead on the basis of machine hours. The question says that activity-based costing should be used. (2) The cost being allocated to Product A is total budgeted overhead costs. The question asks for the amount of receiving costs only to be allocated to Product A, not total overhead costs. 25.

4-11.q: Cost drivers are
o: a mechanical basis, such as machine hours, computer time, size of equipment, or square footage of factory, used to assign costs to activities
accounting techniques used to control costs
activities that cause costs to increase as the activity increases
accounting measurements used to evaluate whether or not performance is proceeding according to plan
4-11.e: Cost drivers are activities that cause costs to be incurred each time the activities occur. A cost driver can be anything at all that causes indirect or overhead costs to arise, as long as those costs can be allocated to individual products or services, or any other entity such as organizational units, in some meaningful way.
wrongWhy: Cost drivers may be used to assign costs, but they do not need to be mechanical. A cost driver is any event or any activity that causes costs to be incurred.
Cost drivers are not accounting techniques used to control costs.
Cost drivers are not accounting measurements used to evaluate whether or not performance is proceeding according to plan. 26.

4-11.q: A company’s operations include a high level of fixed costs and produce a variety of products. What type of costing system should be recommended?
4-11.e: Activity-based costing is a good system to use when there are a lot of fixed costs and a variety of products are produced.
wrongWhy: This is not a costing method. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
Job-order costing is best used when each job is unique and different from others. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
Process costing is best used in a situation in which all of the units go through a very similar production process. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 1.

4-12.topic: Variable and Absorption Costing – Calculation Questions
4-12.q: An exercise equipment manufacturer uses the variable costing method and has determined that the total manufacturing cost of its single 30-pound barbell is $27 per unit. The direct material cost per unit is $16. The allocated administration expenses are $6 per unit. The allocated fixed rental costs are $3 per unit. If no other costs are incurred, the direct labor cost per unit is
4-12.e: Under variable costing, only variable costs are allocated to production. Fixed factory overheads are reported as period costs and are expensed in the period in which they are incurred, so the allocated fixed rental costs are not allocated to units produced. Administration expenses are always expensed as incurred regardless of what method of costing is being used. Therefore, the direct labor cost allocated to each unit under variable costing is the difference between the total manufacturing cost of $27 and the direct material cost of $16 allocated per unit, which is $11.
wrongWhy: This cost for direct labor is incorrect. This is the total manufacturing cost of $27 minus the direct material cost of $16 allocated per unit and minus the allocated administration expenses of $6 per unit. Administration expenses are always period costs that are expensed as incurred, so the administration expenses should not be deducted from the total manufacturing cost to calculate direct labor cost. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This cost for direct labor is incorrect. This is the total manufacturing cost of $27 minus the direct material cost of $16 allocated per unit and minus the allocated fixed rental cost of $3 per unit. Under variable costing, only variable manufacturing costs are allocated to production. Fixed factory overheads are not allocated to units produced under variable costing, so the allocated fixed rental cost should not be deducted from total manufacturing costs to calculate direct labor cost. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This cost for direct labor is incorrect. This is the total manufacturing cost of $27 minus the direct material cost of $16 allocated per unit and minus the allocated fixed rental cost of $3 per unit and minus the allocated administration expenses of $6 per unit. Under variable costing, only variable manufacturing costs are allocated to production. Fixed factory overheads are not allocated to units produced. Administration expenses are always expensed as incurred regardless of what method of costing is being used. So the allocated fixed rental expense and the allocated administration expenses should not be deducted from the total manufacturing cost to calculate the direct labor cost. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 2.

4-12.topic: Variable and Absorption Costing – Calculation Questions
4-12.q: A manufacturer at the end of its fiscal year recorded the data below: If the manufacturer uses variable costing, the inventoriable costs for the fiscal year are:
4-12.e: Under variable costing, the variable costs of production are inventoried while the fixed manufacturing costs are expensed as incurred. The variable costs of production include the prime costs (direct labor and direct materials) and variable manufacturing overhead. In total, this is $900,000.
wrongWhy: This answer includes variable selling and other expenses which are not included in inventoriable costs under the variable method because they are not production costs.
This answer does not include variable manufacturing overhead, which is inventoried under variable costing.
This answer includes fixed manufacturing overhead, which is not inventoried under variable costing. 3.

4-12.topic: Variable and Absorption Costing – Calculation Questions
4-12.q: A manufacturer at the end of its fiscal year recorded the data below: Using absorption (full) costing, inventoriable costs are
4-12.e: Under absorption costing, all manufacturing costs are inventoried. This includes the prime costs (which include direct labor and direct materials), variable manufacturing overhead and fixed manufacturing overhead. In total, this is $1,060,000.
wrongWhy: This answer does not include the manufacturing overheads, which are inventoried under absorption costing.
This answer does not include the fixed manufacturing overhead, which is inventoried under absorption costing.
This answer includes the variable and fixed selling and other expenses (which should not be included) and does not include variable manufacturing overhead (which should be included). 4.

4-12.topic: Variable and Absorption Costing – Calculation Questions
4-12.q: Valyn Corporation employs an absorption costing system for internal reporting purposes; however, the company is considering using variable costing. Data regarding Valyn's planned and actual operations for the calendar year are presented below. Planned Actual Activity Activity Planned Planned Actual Costs Costs Incurred Per Unit Total Costs The planned per unit cost figures shown in the above schedule were based on Valyn producing and selling 140,000 units. Valyn uses a predetermined manufacturing overhead rate for applying manufacturing overhead to its product; thus, a combined manufacturing overhead rate of $9.00 per unit was employed for absorption costing purposes. Any over- or underapplied manufacturing overhead is closed to the Cost of Goods Sold account at the end of the reporting year. The beginning finished goods inventory for absorption costing purposes was valued at the previous year's planned unit manufacturing cost, which was the same as the current year's planned unit manufacturing cost. There are no work-in-process inventories at either the beginning or the end of the year. The planned and actual unit selling price for the current year was $70.00 per unit. The difference between Valyn Corporation's operating income calculated on the absorption costing basis and calculated on the variable costing basis was
4-12.e: This problem does not specify whether Valyn is using a standard cost system (in which standard, or planned, costs are used to account for production) or an actual cost system (in which actual costs are used). However, it does say that Valyn uses a predetermined manufacturing overhead rate for applying manufacturing overhead to its product. And since the actual, incurred per unit costs for direct materials, direct labor and variable manufacturing overhead are exactly the same as the planned per unit costs for those items, we do not need to know whether standard costing or actual costing is being used in order to answer this question. (To verify that the actual variable costs per unit are exactly the same as the planned per unit costs, multiply the planned per unit cost for each of those items by the actual production of 130,000 units. For example, the planned per unit cost for direct materials was $12. Multiplying $12 by the actual activity of 130,000 equals $1,560,000. That $1,560,000 was the flexible budget amount for direct materials, and it is exactly the same as the actual incurred costs.) The difference between absorption costing and variable costing is the treatment of fixed factory overheads. Under the absorption method fixed manufacturing overheads are applied to the units produced at the predetermined rate, whereas under the variable method, they are expensed. The fixed factory overhead cost applied per unit is $5 and we can determine the difference between these two methods by multiplying this $5 per unit difference by the number of units that were added to inventory during the period. Inventory increased by 5,000 units, calculated as follows: Beginning Inventory 35,000 Units + 130,000 Units Produced − 125,000 Units Sold = Ending Inventory 40,000 Units. Thus, the predetermined fixed overhead rate of $5 per unit that was applied to each unit as it was manufactured is attached to the 5,000 units that were added to inventory and was not expensed under absorption costing. 5,000 × $5 gives us a $25,000 difference in income between the two methods. Note that the difference in operating income can be calculated in this way only because 1. The beginning finished goods inventory is valued at the same per unit manufacturing cost as the current year's planned per unit manufacturing cost (which implies that the fixed manufacturing cost in beginning inventory was the same as the current year's predetermined application rate per unit for fixed manufacturing overhead); 2. Over- or under-applied manufacturing overhead is closed to the cost of goods sold account at the end of the period; and 3. There is no beginning work-in-process inventory. If any one of those factors had not been present, we would not have been able to calculate the difference between the two methods in this way.
wrongWhy: This is the amount of underapplied fixed manufacturing overhead. It is not the difference in operating income between the absorption costing and the variable costing methods.

This is (Fixed Manufacturing Cost Per Unit of $5 × Number of units of change in inventory of 5,000) + Underapplied Fixed Manufacturing Overhead of $65,000. The underapplied fixed manufacturing overhead is not relevant to the calculation of the difference in operating income between absorption costing and variable costing, because it is handled the same way under both methods. The $65,000 underapplied fixed manufacturing cost is charged to expense under absorption costing because the problem says that underapplied or overapplied manufacturing overhead is closed out to cost of goods sold. It is also charged to expense under variable costing, because under variable costing, all fixed costs incurred are expensed as period costs as incurred. Since the amount of underapplied fixed manufacturing overhead under absorption costing is handled the same way under both methods, it is not relevant to a calculation of the difference in operating income between them.

This answer results from including fixed administrative expenses along with fixed manufacturing overhead in calculating the fixed cost that would be included in ending inventory under absorption costing but not included under variable costing. Fixed administrative costs (along with fixed selling costs) are expensed in both methods, so the fixed administrative costs do not contribute to the difference between operating income under absorption costing and under variable costing. 6.

4-12.topic: Variable and Absorption Costing – Calculation Questions
4-12.q: Valyn Corporation employs an absorption costing system for internal reporting purposes; however, the company is considering using variable costing. Data regarding Valyn's planned and actual operations for the calendar year are presented below. Planned Actual Activity Activity Planned Planned Actual Costs Costs Incurred Per Unit Total Costs The planned per unit cost figures shown in the above schedule were based on Valyn producing and selling 140,000 units. Valyn uses a predetermined manufacturing overhead rate for applying manufacturing overhead to its product; thus, a combined manufacturing overhead rate of $9.00 per unit was employed for absorption costing purposes. Any over- or underapplied manufacturing overhead is closed to the Cost of Goods Sold account at the end of the reporting year. The beginning finished goods inventory for absorption costing purposes was valued at the previous year's planned unit manufacturing cost, which was the same as the current year's planned unit manufacturing cost. There are no work-in-process inventories at either the beginning or the end of the year. The planned and actual unit selling price for the current year was $70.00 per unit. The total variable cost expensed currently by Valyn Corporation on the variable costing basis was
4-12.e: This problem does not specify whether Valyn is using a standard cost system (in which standard, or planned, costs are used to account for production) or an actual cost system (in which actual costs are used). However, it does say that Valyn uses a predetermined manufacturing overhead rate for applying manufacturing overhead to its product. And since the actual, incurred per unit costs for direct materials, direct labor and variable manufacturing overhead are exactly the same as the planned per unit costs for those items, we do not need to know whether standard costing or actual costing is being used in order to answer this question. (To verify that the actual variable costs per unit are exactly the same as the planned per unit costs, multiply the planned per unit cost for each of those items by the actual production of 130,000 units. For example, the planned per unit cost for direct materials was $12. Multiplying $12 by the actual activity of 130,000 equals $1,560,000. That $1,560,000 was the flexible budget amount for direct materials, and it is exactly the same as the actual incurred costs.) Under variable costing, all variable production costs are put into inventory and are expensed only when the unit is sold. The variable production costs per unit that were applied to the units produced were the costs for direct labor, direct materials and variable overhead, a total of $25 per unit. The number of units sold was 125,000, so the total variable production costs expensed were $3,125,000 ($25 × 125,000). Additionally, the variable selling and administrative costs need to be included in the answer, and those are period costs that are expensed as they are incurred. The variable selling costs were $1,000,000. The variable administrative costs were $250,000. Therefore, the total variable costs expensed were the total variable production costs expensed of $3,125,000, the variable selling costs of $1,000,000, and the variable administrative costs of $250,000, for a total of $4,375,000.
wrongWhy: This is the total of the incurred variable costs. However, variable production costs are put into inventory and are expensed only when the unit is sold. The amount of variable production costs expensed is the actual incurred variable cost per unit produced multiplied by the number of units sold.
This answer results from multiplying the total variable costs per unit by the number of units produced. The number of units sold should be used.

This is not the correct answer. Please see the correct answer for an explanation. We have been unable to determine how to calculate this incorrect answer choice. If you have calculated it, please let us know how you did it so we can create a full explanation of why this answer choice is number and the actual incorrect answer choice -- not its letter, because that can change with every study session created. The Question ID number appears at the top of the question. Thank you in advance for helping us to make your HOCK study materials better. 7.

4-12.topic: Variable and Absorption Costing – Calculation Questions
4-12.q: During the first month of its operations, a company manufactured a product with variable production costs of $50 per unit. Fixed manufacturing overhead totaled $1,800,000 and is allocated based upon units produced. During the month, the company completed 15,000 units, sold 12,000 units, and incurred no variances. If the company’s operating income under absorption costing was $400,000, its operating income (loss) under variable costing was
4-12.e: Under variable costing, the fixed manufacturing costs are fully expensed during the period. Under absorption costing, the fixed manufacturing costs are allocated to the units that are produced. The difference in operating income between the two methods can be calculated by multiplying the amount of fixed overhead per unit by the amount of the change in inventory during the period. During the period, inventory increased. This means that under absorption costing some of the fixed manufacturing costs are included in inventory at the end of the period and have not been expensed. So, we know that because inventory increased during the period the variable method will give a lower operating income. The fixed overhead per unit for the period is $120. This is calculated as the fixed manufacturing amount of $1,800,000 divided by the number of units produced, 15,000.
