Linear programming is a mathematical technique for maximizing or minimizing a given objective subject to certain constraints.
Regression analysis is a statistical procedure for estimating the relation between variables. 5.

4-17.topic: Just-In-Time Systems and MRP, MRPII, ERP
4-17.q: All of the following are benefits of just-in-time manufacturing except that it
o: helps produce a higher-quality product in less time
allows for minor defects without shutting down operations
4-17.e: Just-in-time inventory and manufacturing systems do not allow for minor defects. It is just the opposite. Having a just-in-time system requires elimination of defects and also improves workers' ability to eliminate defects. Because of the close coordination between and among workstations and the minimum inventories held at each workstation when JIT is in use, defects caused at one workstation very quickly affect other workstations. Therefore, JIT requires problems and defects to be solved by eliminating their root causes as quickly as possible. Workers need to be able to trace problems to their source and resolve them at the point where they originated.
wrongWhy: This is a true statement. The goal of a JIT system is to minimize the level of inventories held in the plant at all stages of production, including raw materials, work-in-process, and finished goods inventories while meeting customer demand in a timely manner with high-quality products at the lowest possible cost. The main idea of JIT is that nothing is produced until the next process in the assembly line needs it. In other words, nothing is produced until a customer orders it, and then it is produced very quickly. This "demand-pull" feature of JIT requires close coordination between and among workstations. Close coordination between and among workstations can keep the flow of goods smooth in spite of the low levels of inventory. An important advantage of a JIT system is reduction in the cost of carrying the inventory. In addition to reducing the amount invested in the inventory, the cost savings include reduction in the risk of damage, theft, loss, or a lack of ability to sell the finished goods.

This is a true statement. Having a just-in-time system requires elimination of defects and also improves workers' ability to eliminate defects. Because defects are eliminated, rework costs can also be eliminated. Please see the correct answer for more information.

This is a true statement. Just-in-time inventory and manufacturing systems help in meeting customer demand in a timely manner with high-quality products at the lowest possible cost. Please see the correct answer for more information. 6.

4-17.topic: Just-In-Time Systems and MRP, MRPII, ERP
4-17.q: In a just-in-time-inventory (JIT) system, which statement is false:
o: Because JIT is a push system, production is not driven by the expected demand for the product
The main idea in a JIT system is that the company does not produce anything until the next process requires it
JIT combines purchasing, production and inventory control into one function
4-17.e: The main idea of JIT is that nothing is produced until the next process in the assembly line needs it. JIT is a "pull system" rather than a "push system." In a push system, a department produces and sends all that it can to the next step for further processing, which means that the manufacturer is producing something without understanding consumer demand. This can result in large, useless stocks of inventory. Because the idea of JIT is that nothing will be produced until the next process needs it, technically this means that nothing will be produced until a customer orders it. However, this may not actually be possible in some industries, so in those industries, production is driven by the expected demand for the product.
wrongWhy: This is a true statement.

This is a true statement. JIT is a pull system, which is based on a manufacturing philosophy that combines purchasing, production and inventory control into one function. This reduces the level of inventory that is held within the company at all stages of production, and lowers the cost of carrying the inventory.
This is a true statement. 7.

4-17.topic: Just-In-Time Systems and MRP, MRPII, ERP
4-17.q: An advantage of an enterprise resource planning system is that
o: the system can be easily customized to suit the unique needs of the organization
it can usually be installed by organizations of all sizes
the comprehensiveness of the system reduces resistance to change
4-17.e: One of the advantages of an ERP system is that day-to-day operations are facilitated. All employees can easily gain access to real-time information that they need to do their jobs. Ready access by managers facilitates their decision-making and control over the factors of production. Other advantages of an ERP system are: Operational costs are reduced. Communication is improved across departments, leading to greater efficiencies in production, planning, and decision-making that can lead to lower production costs, lower marketing expenses, and other efficiencies. Inventory management is facilitated. Detailed inventory records are available, simplifying inventory transactions. Inventories can be managed more effectively to keep them at optimal levels. Resource planning as a part of strategic planning is simplified. Senior management has access to the information it needs in order to do strategic planning.
wrongWhy: Some ERP software can be easily customized, and some cannot. Regardless of whether it is customizable or not, customization is not an advantage of an enterprise resource planning system.

It is true that a new generation of ERP systems have become available that are targeted to small and mid-sized businesses. They are easier to install, easier to manage, and require less implementation time and less startup cost than the first generation systems that were practical only for large businesses. This has made ERP systems more accessible to organizations of all sizes. However, that is not an advantage of an enterprise resource planning system.
There is nothing about an ERP system that can have any effect on employees' resistance to change. 8.

4-17.topic: Just-In-Time Systems and MRP, MRPII, ERP
4-17.q: All of the following statements are true about Kanban except
o: Kanban is a part of a JIT system and its purpose is to manage the flow on a manufacturing assembly line
"Kanban" is a Japanese term meaning "continuous improvement."
The supplier delivers components to the production line on an "as needed" basis, signaled by receipt of a card and empty container, eliminating storage in the production area
Kanban was originally developed at Toyota
4-17.e: Kanban is a Japanese term for "visual record." It is a simple parts movement system or an inventory system in which 'cards' or 'tickets' are used to keep track of inventory and its movement. The term "kaizen" is the Japanese term for "continuous improvement."
wrongWhy: Kanban is part of a JIT system and its purpose is to manage the flow on a manufacturing assembly line. This is a chain process where orders flow from one process to another, so production of components is pulled to the production line, rather than the pushed (as is done in the traditional forecast-oriented system).

Kanban is a simple parts movement system or an inventory system in which 'cards' or 'tickets' are used to keep track of inventory and its movement. The supplier delivers components to the production line on an "as needed" basis, signaled by receipt of a card and empty container, eliminating storage in the production area.
Kanban was developed at Toyota. 9.

4-17.topic: Just-In-Time Systems and MRP, MRPII, ERP
4-17.q: A production system in which a unit is produced only when needed in the next step in the production line is called a
4-17.e: The main idea of JIT is that nothing is produced until the next process in the assembly line needs it. In other words, nothing is produced until a customer orders it, and then it is produced very quickly. In a JIT system, inventory is purchased so that it will be delivered just as needed for production. The goal of a JIT system is to minimize the level of inventories held in the plant at all stages of production, including raw materials, work-in-process, and finished goods inventories while meeting customer demand in a timely manner with high-quality products at the lowest possible cost.
wrongWhy: This is not a back-flush system. There is no such thing as a back-flush production system. However, backflush costing is a costing method that is usually used with a just-in-time production system. In backflush costing, the total costs of a production run are recorded in the accounting system only at the end of the process, either when the finished goods are transferred to finished goods inventory, or when they are sold and the expense is recognized as cost of goods sold expense. No work-in-process inventories are carried. Back-flush costing is outside the scope of the CMA exams. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

There is no such system as a Pareto system. The Pareto principle is the 80:20 principle that can be applied to various situations. The Pareto principle states that 80% of consequences come from 20% of the causes. For example, if there is a problem with production, after management pinpoints which 20% of the causes are accounting for 80% of the problems, it can focus its efforts on improving the areas that are likely to have the greatest overall impact. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A Material Requirements Planning (MRP) system is not a production system in which a unit is produced only when needed in the next step in the production line. Material Requirements Planning systems are used to help determine what raw materials to order for production, when to order them, and how much to order. An MRP system can be used with various types of production systems. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 10.

4-17.topic: Just-In-Time Systems and MRP, MRPII, ERP
4-17.q: Successful implementation of a just-in-time (JIT) inventory system requires all of the following except
o: each worker is able to operate all machinery and perform all supporting tasks
close coordination between and among work stations
use of Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) software
4-17.e: Enterprise Resource Planning software integrates all departments and functions across a company onto a single computer system with a single database so that the information needed by all areas of the company will be available to the people who need it for planning, manufacturing, order fulfillment, and other purposes. Use of Enterprise Resource Planning software is not required in order to implement a Just- In-Time (JIT) inventory system.
wrongWhy: The company must be reorganized to permit lean manufacturing, where the plant layout is arranged by manufacturing cells or work cells that produce a product, or product type. Each worker in a manufacturing cell knows how to operate all machines in that cell and can perform supporting tasks within that cell, which lessens downtime resulting from breakdowns or employee absences.

The company must be reorganized along lean manufacturing lines, to permit close coordination between and among work stations. The plant layout is arranged by manufacturing cells or work cells that each produce a product, or product type.
Close relationships with suppliers are required because inventory levels are kept low. The suppliers must make frequent deliveries of smaller amounts of inventory. 11.

4-17.topic: Just-In-Time Systems and MRP, MRPII, ERP
4-17.q: A company is planning to implement a just-in-time (JIT) inventory management system. All of the following are benefits expected from implementing JIT inventory management except
o: lower obsolescence and other carrying costs
4-17.e: When a just-in-time inventory management system is utilized, inventory (either raw materials inventory or, for a retailer, completed goods) is purchased just as needed and is immediately used, either transferred to production or sold to the end user. The JIT inventory management system does not involve the manufacturing part of JIT. Therefore, fewer defects and less rework are not a benefit of a JIT inventory management system, though they are required on the production side of a JIT system and so are a benefit of JIT production.
wrongWhy: Reduction in the amount of inventory carried and thus a reduction in the amount of investment in inventory is an advantage of a JIT inventory management system.
Reduction in the amount of inventory carried and thus reductions in inventory obsolescence and minimization of other carrying costs are advantages of a JIT inventory management system.
Reduction in the amount of inventory carried is an advantage of a JIT inventory management system. When inventory is reduced, higher inventory turnover results. 1.

4-17.q: An ERP system differs from traditional separate systems primarily because ERP:
o: Integrates all business functions into one unified database
Is used only for manufacturing companies
Replaces the need for management accountants
4-17.e: ERP integrates All business functions (finance, manufacturing, HR, sales, procurement) into One unified database, eliminating data silos and providing real-time, consistent information across the entire organization.

4-17.q: The Master Production Schedule (Mps) in MRP specifies:
o: The list of all materials needed per product
What finished products to produce and when
4-17.e: The Mps specifies What finished products to produce, in what Quantities, and When. It is the primary input to MRP, which uses the Mps to calculate material requirements.

4-17.q: The Bill of Materials (Bom) contains:
o: The complete list of components and quantities needed to make one unit of a product
4-17.e: Bom = complete list of All components, subassemblies, and raw materials (with quantities) needed to produce One unit of the finished product. It is a key input to MRP calculations.

4-17.q: Gross Requirements 500 units. On-hand inventory 120. Scheduled receipts 80. Net Requirements =
4-17.e: Net Requirements = Gross - On Hand - Scheduled Receipts = 500 - 120 - 80 = 300 units need to be ordered or produced.

4-17.q: MRP Ii extends MRP by adding:
o: Capacity planning, labor scheduling, and financial integration
4-17.e: MRP Ii extends MRP beyond materials to include Capacity Planning (are machines/labor available?), labor scheduling, machine scheduling, and financial planning — integrating manufacturing with the broader business.

4-17.q: MRP is classified as a _____ production system:
4-17.e: MRP is a Push system — production is based on Forecasts and schedules. Products are pushed through production based on planned demand. JIT is a Pull system — production is triggered by actual customer demand.

4-17.q: The Biggest risk of implementing an ERP system is:
o: The system will automate too many jobs
Implementation failure causing cost overruns and business disruption
The system will not connect to the internet
Accounting standards will not be followed
4-17.e: ERP implementation failure is a major risk — projects frequently exceed budget (sometimes by 100%+), take longer than planned, and can disrupt business operations. Many high-profile ERP failures have caused significant financial damage to companies.

4-17.q: Which of the following is an example of an ERP system?
4-17.e: Sap S/4HANA is a major ERP system. Other examples: Oracle ERP Cloud, Microsoft Dynamics 365. QuickBooks is a small business accounting system (not a full ERP). Excel is a spreadsheet tool.

4-17.q: In an MRP system, 'Scheduled Receipts' refers to:
o: Orders already placed and expected to arrive
4-17.e: Scheduled Receipts = orders already placed with suppliers (or already in production) that are expected to Arrive during the planning period. They reduce net requirements because they represent incoming supply.

4-18.q: Nash Glassworks Company has budgeted fixed factory overhead of $100,000 per month. The company uses absorption costing for both external and internal financial reporting purposes. Budgeted factory overhead rates for cost allocations for the month of April using alternative unit output denominator levels are shown below. Budgeted Denominator Budgeted Capacity Level Overhead Levels (units of output) Cost Rate Actual output for the month of April was 800,000 units of glassware. When Nash Glassworks Company allocates fixed costs, management will select a capacity level to use as the denominator volume. All of the following would be appropriate as the capacity level that approximates actual volume levels except
4-18.e: Theoretical capacity is the absolute maximum that can be produced. This would not approximate the actual volume levels.
wrongWhy: This is the expected level of output for the year so this would approximate the actual volume.
Expected annual activity would essentially be the same as the master budget level and this is would approximate the actual volume.
Normal capacity is the long-term average, so this would approximate the actual volume. 2.

4-18.q: Nash Glassworks Company has budgeted fixed factory overhead of $100,000 per month. The company uses absorption costing for both external and internal financial reporting purposes. Budgeted factory overhead rates for cost allocations for the month of April using alternative unit output denominator levels are shown below. Budgeted Denominator Budgeted Capacity Level Overhead Levels (units of output) Cost Rate Actual output for the month of April was 800,000 units of glassware. The choice of a production volume level as a denominator in the computation of fixed overhead rates can significantly affect reported net income. Which one of the following statements is correct for Nash Glassworks Company if its beginning inventory is zero, production exceeded sales, and variances are adjustments to cost of goods sold?
o: Normal capacity as the denominator level will result in a lower net income amount than if any other capacity volume is chosen
Practical capacity as the denominator level will result in a lower net income amount than if master-budget capacity is chosen
Practical capacity as the denominator level will result in a higher net income amount than if normal capacity is chosen
Master-budget capacity as the denominator level will result in a lower net income amount than if theoretical capacity is chosen
4-18.e: The most important fact given here is that variances are adjustments to cost of goods sold. If variances are prorated among cost of goods sold, finished goods inventory and work in process inventory, the choice of a denominator level will make no difference in net income, because net income will be the same no matter which denominator level is chosen. However, when all variances are adjustments to cost of goods sold, the choice of the denominator level will make a difference in net income. By using the practical level, a smaller amount of overhead will go to each unit than if the master-budget level is used. Therefore, less overhead will be included in ending inventory at the end of the period. Since there is less overhead on the balance sheet, there will be more overhead on the income statement since variances go to cost of goods sold. More overhead on the income statement will lead to a lower net income.
wrongWhy: Because the normal capacity has the highest overhead rate, more overhead will be included in inventory using this capacity than any other. Therefore, using normal capacity will lead to a higher income than any of the other methods.

Because the practical capacity is higher than the normal capacity, the normal capacity level will lead to higher overhead costs per unit. Therefore, the normal capacity level will lead to more overhead being included in inventory and a higher income than the practical capacity would give.

Because the master-budget capacity is less than theoretical, the master-budget capacity will allocate more overhead to each unit. This means that there will be more overhead costs in inventory using the master-budget capacity and therefore a higher net income. 3.

4-18.q: The appropriate method for the disposition of underapplied or overapplied factory overhead
o: Is to cost of goods sold only
Depends on the significance of the amount
Is to finished goods inventory only
Is apportioned to cost of goods sold and finished goods inventory
4-18.e: The treatment of the underapplied or overapplied overhead depends on the materiality of the amount. If it is immaterial it can be moved to cost of goods sold. If it is material, it is apportioned among work in process inventory, finished goods inventory, and cost of goods sold according to the amount of overhead included in each that was allocated to the current period’s production.
wrongWhy: If the amount of underapplied or overapplied overhead is immaterial, it can be charged to cost of goods sold. However, if the amount is material, it is prorated among work in process and finished goods inventories and cost of goods sold.
Underapplied or overapplied overhead is never allocated only to finished goods inventory.
If the amount is material, the underapplied or overapplied overhead is apportioned to work in process inventory, finished goods inventory, and cost of goods sold. 4.

4-18.q: A company had the following applied fixed overhead balances at the end of its first year in business. The company is also showing that it has materially under-applied fixed overhead by $30,000. To record the fixed overhead variance, the company should charge
4-18.e: At the end of a period, if the amount of overhead that was over- or under-applied is material, it must be distributed among the WIP inventories, finished goods inventory, and cost of goods sold accounts. The variances are pro-rated according to the amount of overhead included in each that was applied to the current period’s production, not according to the ending balances in each. Since this company has completed its first year in business, the balances in each account can be used. The $30,000 under-application of overhead is pro-rated as follows: Overhead Percentage Pro-Rated Included of Total Amount Work-in-process inventory $100,000 25.0% $ 7,500 Finished goods inventory 50,000 12.5% 3,750 Cost of goods sold 250,000 62.5% 18,750 Total $400,000 $30,000 Finished goods inventory will be charged for $3,750.
wrongWhy: Cost of goods sold will be charged for $18,750, not $7,500. Please see the correct answer explanation for information on how the amount is calculated.
Work-in-process inventory will be charged for $7,500, not $18,750. Please see the correct answer explanation for information on how the amount is calculated.

At the end of a period, if the amount of overhead that was over- or under-applied is material, it must be distributed among the WIP inventories, finished goods inventory, and cost of goods sold accounts. Therefore, cost of goods sold will be charged for $18,750, not the full under- applied amount of $30,000. Please see the correct answer explanation for information on how the amount is calculated. 5.

4-18.q: Practical capacity as a plant capacity concept
o: assumes all personnel and equipment will operate at peak efficiency and total plant capacity will be used
includes consideration of idle time caused by both limited sales orders and human and equipment inefficiencies
is the production volume that is necessary to meet sales demand for the next year
does not consider idle time caused by inadequate sales demand
4-18.e: Practical capacity is the theoretical capacity reduced by allowances for downtime but not reduced for any idle time caused by a decrease in sales demand.
wrongWhy: Practical capacity assumes that there will be some down time in the factory production as a result of human and machine inefficiencies.
Practical capacity includes downtime from human and equipment inefficiencies, but not from the lack of demand.
This is not related to practical capacity — it is just the requirement needed to meet demand. 1.

4-18.q: Which capacity concept represents the maximum output under perfect conditions with no downtime?
4-18.e: Theoretical Capacity = maximum output 24/7 under Perfect conditions — no breakdowns, no scheduled maintenance, no employee breaks. It is a theoretical benchmark never actually achieved in practice.

4-18.q: A sunk cost is:
o: A future cost relevant to the decision
A past cost that cannot be changed — always irrelevant
A variable cost that changes with production
4-18.e: Sunk costs are Past costs already incurred that cannot be recovered regardless of the decision made. They are Always Irrelevant in decision-making because they do not differ between alternatives.

4-18.q: Outside purchase price $18/unit. Variable cost to make $14/unit. Avoidable fixed costs $3/unit. Should the company make or buy?
4-18.e: Avoidable cost to make = $14 variable + $3 avoidable fixed = $17/unit. Outside price = $18. Make costs Less ($17 < $18) → Make and save $1/unit.

4-18.q: A special order should be Accepted when:
o: The special price exceeds the full absorption cost
The special price exceeds variable cost And excess capacity exists
The special price equals the regular selling price
Fixed costs are covered by the special order
4-18.e: Accept special order if: Special price > Variable cost per unit And there is Excess Capacity. Fixed costs are irrelevant (already covered by regular sales). The order contributes positive Cm.

4-18.q: When deciding to Drop a product line, which costs are Irrelevant?
4-18.e: Allocated (common) fixed costs are Irrelevant in keep-or-drop decisions because they continue regardless of whether the segment is dropped — they will just be reallocated to remaining segments. Only Avoidable costs matter.

4-18.q: When a company has a scarce resource (constraint), products should be ranked by:
o: Highest Cm per unit of the scarce resource
4-18.e: With a constraint, rank by Cm Per Unit Of The Scarce Resource. A product with lower Cm per unit may be more profitable if it uses less of the constrained resource. Example: Cm $10 using 5 machine hours = $2/Mh vs Cm $8 using 2 Mh = $4/Mh → prefer the second.

4-18.q: Opportunity cost is:
o: A sunk cost from a previous decision
The cost of the next best alternative foregone
Equal to the variable cost of production
4-18.e: Opportunity cost = the benefit Given Up by choosing one alternative over the next best option. It is Always relevant because it represents the true economic cost of a decision. Example: using a machine for Product A means foregoing the contribution margin from Product B.

4-18.q: Normal capacity is used to calculate Pohr because:
o: It is the maximum possible capacity
It smooths out seasonal fluctuations over a longer period
4-18.e: Normal capacity (2-3 year average) is used for Pohr because it smooths out seasonal and cyclical fluctuations, providing a stable overhead rate. Using actual capacity would cause overhead rates to fluctuate wildly between periods.

4-18.q: Variable cost $20/unit. Fixed costs $50,000 (total). Special order 1,000 units at $24/unit. Excess capacity exists. Accept or reject?
o: Accept — only if fixed costs are waived
4-18.e: Special order analysis: Price ($24) > Variable cost ($20) → Cm = $4/unit. Total contribution = $4 x 1,000 = $4,000. Fixed costs are Irrelevant (already covered). Accept the order — it adds $4,000 to profit.

4-18.q: Product A: Cm $30, requires 3 machine hours. Product B: Cm $40, requires 5 machine hours. Machine hours are the constraint. Which product should be prioritized?
o: Product B — higher Cm per unit
Product A — higher Cm per machine hour
Product B — requires more machine hours
4-18.e: Product A: Cm per Mh = $30/3 = $10/Mh. Product B: Cm per Mh = $40/5 = $8/Mh. Product A generates More contribution margin per machine hour → Prioritize Product A when machine hours are constrained.

4-18.q: Practical Capacity differs from Theoretical Capacity because Practical Capacity:
o: Subtracts normal downtime, maintenance, and breaks
Is used for tax reporting only
4-18.e: Practical Capacity = Theoretical Capacity - Normal downtime (scheduled maintenance, employee breaks, holidays). It is a Realistic maximum achievable output. Theoretical capacity is a benchmark never truly achievable in practice.

4-18.q: Normal Capacity is used for Pohr because it:
o: Smooths seasonal fluctuations over 2-3 years for a stable rate
4-18.e: Normal Capacity (2-3 year average) is used for Pohr because it Smooths seasonal and cyclical fluctuations, providing a Stable overhead rate throughout the year. Using actual capacity would cause overhead rates to fluctuate wildly.

4-18.q: In a Make-or-Buy decision, allocated fixed costs are:
4-18.e: Allocated (common) fixed costs are Irrelevant in Make-or-Buy — they continue even if the company outsources (just reallocated elsewhere). Only Avoidable costs matter — costs that actually disappear if production stops.

4-18.q: A special order should be Rejected when:
o: Price exceeds variable cost with excess capacity
4-18.e: Reject a special order if: Special Price < Variable Cost per unit. If the price does not even cover variable costs, each unit sold Loses money. Accept only if Price > Variable Cost And excess capacity is available.

4-18.q: In Keep-or-Drop decisions, which cost is Always irrelevant?
4-18.e: Allocated (common) fixed costs are Always irrelevant in Keep-or-Drop — they do not disappear when the segment is dropped. They are simply reallocated to remaining segments. Only avoidable costs and lost Cm matter.

4-19.q: A manufacturing company incurred the following costs during the last quarter, related to product development and production. Materials storage $ 50,000 What amount of costs should be classified as value-added?
4-19.e: Only the product design ($180,000) and direct materials ($400,000) costs are value- added costs.
wrongWhy: Material storage and materials handling and moving are not value-added costs.
Quality inspections are not a value-added cost.
Materials handling and moving and quality inspections are not value-added costs. 2.

4-19.q: A company desires to prepare two sets of financial statements. Conventional financial statements would be prepared along with a set that is totally consistent with value-chain analysis. How would customer service costs be treated in the two statements?
o: As a noninventoriable cost in conventional financial statements; and as a product cost in value-chain financial statements
As an inventoriable cost in conventional financial statements; and as a non- product cost in value-chain financial statements
As a noninventoriable cost in conventional financial statements; and as a non- product cost in value-chain financial statements
As an inventoriable cost in conventional financial statements; and as a product cost in value-chain financial statements
4-19.e: Customer service occurs after the sale and cannot be inventoried. It is however, part of the value of the product and is considered a product cost in a value-chain statement.
wrongWhy: Customer service occurs after the sale and cannot be inventoried. Customer service is also a product cost, not a non-product cost.
Customer service is a product cost, not a non-product cost.
Customer service occurs after the sale and cannot be inventoried. 3.

4-19.q: The value chain is made up of both primary and support activities. Of the following, which is a primary activity?
4-19.e: Marketing and Sales is a primary activity. Marketing and sales includes advertising, promotion and sales activities. The other answer choices are all support activities.
wrongWhy: Infrastructure is a support activity.
Human Resources is a support activity.
Information Systems is a support activity. 4.

4-19.q: One of the key sources of competitive advantage is:
o: Slow adoption of more efficient business practices
Taking advantage of, and being a follower in, competitors' product innovation
4-19.e: In analyzing why some companies outperform others, we see that it comes in great part from their pursuit of responsiveness to customers' needs and customer satisfaction.
wrongWhy: This could detract from gaining and sustaining competitive advantage.
This could detract from gaining and sustaining competitive advantage.
This could detract from gaining and sustaining competitive advantage. 5.

4-19.q: Value chain analysis can help an organization gain competitive advantage by identifying what does or does not increase value to the customers. Which of the following is not one of the three steps in value chain analysis?
o: Build competitive advantage by either increasing value to the customer or reducing the costs of production
Determine the amount of value that customers attribute to the end product
Identify the activities that add value to the end product
Identify the cost driver or cost drivers for each activity
4-19.e: This is not a step in value chain analysis.
wrongWhy: This is a step in value chain analysis.
This is a step in value chain analysis. What the specific activities are will depend upon the industry and what the company does (manufacturing, resale, etc.).
This is a step in value chain analysis. 6.

4-19.q: An outside consultant has been hired by a manufacturing firm to evaluate each of the firm’s major products beginning with the design of the products and continuing through the manufacture, warehousing, distribution, sales, and service. The consultant has also been requested to compare the manufacturer’s major products with firms that are manufacturing and marketing the same or similar products. The consultant is to identify where customer value can be increased, where costs can be reduced, and to provide a better understanding of the linkages with customers, suppliers, and other firms in the industry. The type of analysis that the consultant most likely has been asked to perform for the manufacturing firm is called a
4-19.e: Because the analysis is to include identifying where customer value can be increased, the type of analysis the consultant most likely has been asked to perform is a value-chain analysis.
wrongWhy: The analysis described is not a SWOT analysis.
The analysis described is not a benchmarking analysis.
The analysis described is not a balanced scorecard analysis. 7.

4-19.q: Consider the following categories of performance measures. I. Profitability measures. II. Customer-satisfaction measures. III. Efficiency, quality, and time measures. IV. Innovation measures. A cruise line operates on a national scale in a very competitive marketplace. In view of this information, which measures should the company use in the evaluation of its managers?
4-19.e: These categories of performance measures are all things that a company needs in order to have competitive advantage. Competitive advantage is an advantage that a company has over its competitors which it gains by offering consumers greater value than they can get from its competitors. In a very competitive marketplace such as this cruise line is in, competitive advantage is essential. In order to have competitive advantage, the company needs to excel in all of these performance measures. If managers are not evaluated on their performance in these areas, they will not work toward excelling in them, and the company will not have competitive advantage. Therefore, the company should use all of these measures in its evaluation of its managers.
wrongWhy: Evaluating managers on customer-satisfaction measures and efficiency, quality and time measures is important, but these are not the only things they should be evaluated on.
Evaluating managers on profitability measures is important, but it is not the only thing they should be evaluated on.
Evaluating managers on profitability measures and customer-satisfaction measures is important, but these are not the only things they should be evaluated on. 8.

4-19.q: Which one of the following lists of functions is in proper value chain order?
o: Research and development, marketing, and customer service
Research and development, customer service, and distribution
4-19.e: Research and development, marketing, and customer service is the correct order of those functions in the value chain. The primary activities in the value chain, in order of their completion, are: research & development, production, marketing & sales, and customer services. Research provides a company with needed knowledge for creating new or improving existing products, services or processes. Development uses those research findings to develop and improve the company’s products, processes, or services. The next step is design, which is the detailed planning and engineering for these efforts. Design is followed by production, which is the acquisition, coordination and assembly required to produce a product or deliver a service. Once the product is available for sale, marketing and sales, including advertising, promotion and sales activities, create the sales. Distribution involves delivery of products or services to customers after the sales are made. Customer service includes customer support and warranty services after a sale takes place.
wrongWhy: Distribution involves delivery of products or services to customers after the sales are made, and customer service cannot be provided until the goods or services are in the hands of the purchasers.
Production design would logically have to come before production.

Distribution involves delivery of products or services to customers after the sales are made. Marketing efforts are required to make the sales. So marketing would logically have to come before distribution. 9.

4-19.q: The primary activities or business functions that are considered in the value chain include:
o: Customer service, production, marketing and sales, and R&D
Customer service, production, marketing and sales, and human resources
Customer service, production, marketing and sales, and information systems
Infrastructure, human resources, materials management, and R&D
4-19.e: Customer service, production, marketing and sales, and R&D are the primary activities or business functions that add value to a company’s product or service. Information systems, infrastructure, human resources, and materials management are activities that support the primary activities as defined in the value chain.
wrongWhy: Human resources is a secondary value chain activities.
Information systems is a secondary value chain activities.
Infrastructure, human resources, and materials management are secondary value chain activities. 1.

4-19.q: When evaluating an outsourcing decision, which costs are Relevant?
o: All fixed costs including allocated overhead
Avoidable variable and fixed costs only
4-19.e: Only Avoidable costs are relevant in outsourcing decisions — costs that will be eliminated if production stops (variable costs + avoidable fixed costs). Allocated/unavoidable fixed costs continue regardless and are Irrelevant.

4-19.q: Variable cost to make $25/unit. Avoidable fixed cost $3/unit. Allocated fixed cost $5/unit. Outside price $30. Should the company outsource?
4-19.e: Avoidable cost to make = $25 + $3 = $28/unit (allocated $5 is irrelevant). Outside price = $30. Keep making ($28 < $30) — outsourcing costs $2 More per unit.

4-19.q: A company should generally Not outsource activities that are:
4-19.e: Core Competencies — activities that provide strategic competitive advantage — should Not be outsourced. Outsourcing them risks losing the capability, sharing proprietary knowledge with competitors, and losing strategic differentiation.

4-19.q: A cost leadership strategy focuses on:
o: Offering unique products customers pay premium for
Being the lowest-cost producer in the industry
Maximizing product quality regardless of cost
4-19.e: Cost Leadership strategy = being the Lowest-Cost Producer. Companies like Walmart pursue cost leadership by aggressively reducing costs through efficiency, economies of scale, and tight cost control throughout the value chain.

4-19.q: A differentiation strategy implies that cost management should:
o: Invest in costs that support the unique value offered to customers
4-19.e: Differentiation strategy: Invest in costs that support the unique value customers pay a premium for — quality, R&D, customer service, brand. Cutting these costs would undermine the differentiation and destroy competitive advantage.

4-19.q: Customer profitability analysis assigns costs to customers based on:
4-19.e: Customer profitability uses ABC principles to assign costs based on Resources Consumed by each customer — ordering frequency, returns, customer service calls, special requirements, etc. Some customers are highly profitable; others are unprofitable despite high revenue.

4-19.q: The Balanced Scorecard's Financial perspective answers the question:
o: What must we excel at internally?
How do we look to shareholders?
4-19.e: Financial perspective asks: 'How do we look to Shareholders?' — focusing on financial results like revenue growth, profitability, and ROI. The other perspectives are Customer ('How do customers see us?'), Internal Process ('What must we excel at?'), and Learning & Growth ('Can we continue to improve?').

4-19.q: Supply chain disruption risk is a major Qualitative factor against:
4-19.e: Supply chain disruption is a key Risk Of Outsourcing. When an external supplier fails to deliver (due to financial problems, natural disasters, capacity issues), the buying company has no backup production capability. This is especially critical for JIT manufacturers.

4-19.q: Accounting process redesign under lean primarily aims to:
o: Increase the number of journal entries for greater accuracy
Eliminate non-value-added accounting tasks and align reporting with lean operations
Require more detailed monthly variance analysis
Expand standard costing to all departments
4-19.e: Lean accounting process redesign eliminates non-value-added accounting work (unused reports, excessive transactions), simplifies recording (backflush costing), and aligns the accounting system with lean operations — so accounting Supports lean behaviour rather than contradicting it.

4-19.q: Traditional standard costing can conflict with lean manufacturing because it:
o: Rewards production volume — managers produce more to absorb fixed overhead, building up inventory
4-19.e: Traditional absorption costing allocates fixed overhead per unit — so Producing More units lowers unit cost and improves reported performance. This incentivises Overproduction and inventory build-up, directly contradicting lean's goal of zero inventory and pull-based production.

4-19.q: Backflush costing supports lean/JIT because it:
o: Records costs at every stage of production separately
Records costs only at completion or sale — eliminating complex Wip tracking
Increases the number of accounting transactions
4-19.e: Backflush costing Delays cost recording until products are completed or sold, then 'flushes' costs back. This eliminates the complex, time-consuming tracking of costs through multiple Wip stages — supporting JIT's goal of simplified, streamlined operations.

4-20.q: Retail Partners Inc., which operates eight discount store chains, is seeking to reduce the costs of its purchasing activities through reengineering and a heavier use of electronic data interchange (EDI). Which of the following benchmarking techniques would be appropriate in this situation? I. A comparison of the purchasing costs and practices of each of Retail Partners’ store chains to identify their internal "best in class." II. A comparison of the practices of Retail Partners to those of Discount City, another retailer, whose practices are often considered "best in class." III. A comparison of the practices of Retail Partners to those of Capital Airways, an international airline, whose practices are often considered "best in class." IV. An in-depth review of a retail trade association publication on successful electronic data interchange applications.
4-20.e: A benchmark is a standard of performance, and benchmarking is the process of measuring the organization against the products, practices and services of some of its most efficient global competitors. The company can use these standards as a target or model for its own operations. This is called "best practices," or "best in class." A benchmark can also be an internal "best practice." Through the application of research and sophisticated software analysis tools, companies can do best practice analysis to cut costs, boost output, and achieve their strategic business goals and objectives. Benchmarking continuously strives to imitate the performance of the best performers, and by meeting these high standards, the organization may be able to create a competitive advantage over its marketplace competitors. The benchmarked company does not need to be in the same industry as the company that is trying to improve its performance.
wrongWhy: These are correct, but this is not the best answer. A review of successes in other industries and research into other companies’ experiences can provide ideas as well.
These are correct, but this is not the best answer. A review of the competition as well as successes in other industries can provide ideas as well.
These are correct, but this is not the best answer. A review of in-house best practices as well as successes in other industries can provide ideas as well. 2.

4-20.q: Stumphouse Cheese is in the process of implementing a cost improvement system with kaizen costing as the basis for budgeting all manufacturing activities. This will be utilized over the next four years in an attempt to become more profitable. The target reduction rate has been set at 5% of fixed overhead costs. Total fixed overhead costs for this year were $900,000. What is the budgeted amount for the next two years using kaizen costing? Current Year +1, Current Year +2
4-20.e: Current Year +1 will be 95% of the current year's fixed overhead costs, which is $900,000 × 0.95 = $855,000. Current Year +2 will be 95% of Current Year +1's fixed overhead costs, which is $855,000 × 0.95 = $812,250
wrongWhy: Current Year +1 is calculated correctly, but Current Year +2 uses the same $45,000 that was deducted from the Current Year cost to calculate the Current Year +1 cost. Instead, the 5% reduction should be calculated on the Current Year +1 costs.
The cost reductions should begin in Current Year +1.
Costs for the Current Year +1 are calculated correctly; but Current Year +2 can be determined. 3.

4-20.q: Which of the following statements is true about benchmarking?
o: Benchmarking team members should be made up only of senior managers responsible for the strategic planning and management of the company
After best practice analysis, a company must then identify the critical success factors for its business
Companies undertake best practice analysis to cut costs, boost output, and achieve their strategic business goals and objectives
Benchmarking is the process of measuring the organization against the products, practices and services of all of its most efficient global competitors
4-20.e: The company can use these standards as a target or model for its own operations. This is also called best practices. Through the application of research and sophisticated software analysis tools, companies undertake best practice analysis to cut costs, boost output, and achieve their strategic business goals and objectives.
wrongWhy: Team members should be from different areas of the business and have different skills.
Identification of critical success factors is a first step.

A benchmark is a standard of performance, and benchmarking is the process of measuring the organization against the products, practices and services of some, but not particularly all, of its most efficient global competitors. 4.

4-20.q: Pavilion Inc. has implemented a budget process that begins with the analysis of current practices to find improvements and determine changes needed to attain improvements. Then budgets are based on the improved practices or procedures resulting in budget figures that are lower than the previous period. The firm expects to be able to manufacture its product or render its service at a lower cost. The decrease in the budget amounts are the consequence of doing the same activity more efficiently and with higher quality and is not the result of arbitrary cuts. The budget process described is referred to as:
4-20.e: Kaizen is a Japanese term for continuous improvement. When applied to the budgeting process, the philosophy of kaizen leads to expected continuous improvements in the production process. A budget developed on the kaizen philosophy will take into account these expected improvements.
wrongWhy: Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting method in which the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period's budget and the likely operating results for the current period. Zero based budgeting does not refer to or use current practices, but starts all over. The budget described here starts by looking at the current practices and then at ways to improve current practices and lower current costs.

Activity-based budgeting is similar in concept to activity-based costing. Activities that drive the costs are identified, a budgeted level of activity for each of these drivers is determined based on a budgeted level of production, and budgeted amounts are developed based on the budgeted level of activity. This focuses on activities but not necessarily on improvement of performance.

Standard cost budgeting uses standard costs and a flexible budget that can be adjusted to reflect actual production. However, standard cost budgeting looks more at expected costs rather than improvement of activities and improvement of related costs. 5.

4-20.q: From the perspective of the management accountant, which one of the following represents a major disadvantage of business process reengineering?
o: It often results in a decreased use of centralized databases
It results in heavier maintenance for legacy systems
Internal control mechanisms are often disassembled
The focus is, to a large extent, on short-term results
4-20.e: Business process reengineering involves analyzing and radically redesigning the work flow. Radical redesign means throwing out the old procedures and inventing new ways of getting the work done. As a business process is disassembled in order to be restructured, the internal controls that have been developed for the process will need to be revised or completely rewritten in order to effectively control the restructured process. If that step is overlooked, management will have no internal controls over the new process, leaving the company vulnerable to failure to achieve its objectives.
wrongWhy: One of the basic premises of business process reengineering is that information should be captured only once, and it should appear simultaneously every place it is needed. If someone is taking information from one system and inputting it into another, that is a process that is broken and needs to be reengineered. Therefore, business process reengineering is likely to result in an increased use of centralized databases.

A legacy system is defined as a computer system that has been in operation for a long time, and whose functions are too essential to be disrupted by upgrading or integration with another system. Business process reengineering involves analyzing and radically redesigning the work flow. Radical redesign means throwing out the old procedures and inventing new ways of getting the work done. A reengineered process would not use a legacy system but instead would require a new system.
The focus of business process reengineering is on long-term results. 6.

4-20.q: A company's market share and profitability has slowly been eroding the past few years. The board and management realize that some type of change is needed, but they are unsure what type of change is needed, whether incremental or more drastic and fundamental, such as business process reengineering (BPR). Which of the following situations would indicate that the change needs to be drastic and fundamental?
o: Shareholders want higher returns on their investments
The company has a legacy IT system
A main competitor has a restocking time of 36 hours versus the company's two weeks
The company's credit-lending program needs to reduce credit losses by 10% to be in line with its competitors
4-20.e: If a competitor has a restocking time of 36 hours versus two weeks, this gives the competitor a huge cost advantage. That is an indication that a drastic and fundamental change is needed, and business process engineering is a way to accomplish that. Business process reengineering involves analyzing and radically redesigning the workflow. Radical redesign means throwing out the old procedures and inventing new ways of getting the work done. Reengineering is not about making incremental improvements. It is about making quantum leaps.
wrongWhy: If the company can reverse the erosion of market share and profitability, shareholder returns should improve. But a goal of improving shareholder returns does not necessarily indicate that changes need to be drastic and fundamental, nor does it help the company's management and board to determine where to direct their efforts to improve market share and profitability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Operating a legacy IT system does not indicate that a drastic and fundamental change is needed. Furthermore, replacing the IT system it would not improve eroding market share and profitability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A need to reduce credit losses by 10% does not indicate that a drastic and fundamental change is required. Furthermore, reducing credit losses would not improve the company's market share, and any reduction in credit loss expense that would result would have a minimal effect on profitability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 1.

4-20.q: Takt Time is calculated as:
o: Total production time / Number of workers
Available production time / Customer demand rate
Cycle time x Number of units
4-20.e: Takt Time = Available Production Time / Customer Demand Rate. It sets the Production Pace needed to meet customer demand. If takt time = 4 minutes, one unit must be completed every 4 minutes.

4-20.q: Available time 600 minutes/day. Customer demand 150 units/day. Takt Time =
4-20.e: Takt Time = 600 / 150 = 4 minutes per unit. The production process must complete one unit every 4 minutes to exactly meet customer demand without overproducing.

4-20.q: Lead Time differs from Cycle Time because Lead Time:
o: Includes total time from order to delivery, including waiting
Is always shorter than cycle time
4-20.e: Lead Time = Total time from customer order to delivery — including all waiting, inspection, transport, and processing time. Cycle Time = time to complete one unit in production. Lead Time is almost always longer than Cycle Time due to non-value-added waiting.

4-20.q: Value Stream Mapping (Vsm) shows:
o: Both material and information flows from supplier to customer
4-20.e: Vsm maps the Entire flow of Both materials (physical product flow) And information (orders, schedules, signals) from supplier through the company to the customer. It reveals waste in the entire value stream.

4-20.q: The Value-Added Ratio is calculated as:
4-20.e: Value-Added Ratio = Process Time / Lead Time. It shows what percentage of total lead time is actually spent adding value. A ratio of 10% means only 10% of time is value-added — 90% is waste (waiting, transport, inspection, etc.).

4-20.q: In process analysis, a 'Current State Map' documents:
o: The process as it exists Today — including all waste
4-20.e: Current State Map documents the process As IT IS Today — including all waste, inefficiencies, waiting time, excess inventory, and non-value-added activities. It is the starting point for identifying improvement opportunities.

4-20.q: The Bottleneck in a process is:
o: The slowest step that limits overall throughput
The step with the most workers
4-20.e: The bottleneck is the Slowest step — the constraint that limits the rate of the entire system. Overall throughput cannot exceed the bottleneck's capacity. All upstream processes accumulate inventory waiting for the bottleneck.

4-20.q: Improving a non-bottleneck process step will:
o: Have no effect on overall throughput
4-20.e: Improving a Non-Bottleneck step does Not increase overall throughput — the bottleneck still limits output. It may create more inventory piling up in front of the bottleneck. Only improving the Bottleneck increases system throughput.

4-20.q: The correct order of the 5 Toc steps is:
4-20.e: Toc 5 Steps: (1) Identify the constraint, (2) Exploit — get maximum output from it, (3) Subordinate — align all other processes to support it, (4) Elevate — invest to increase its capacity, (5) Repeat — find the next constraint.

4-20.q: Under throughput costing, which costs are treated as Product costs?
o: Direct Materials and Direct Labor only
Direct Materials, Direct Labor, and Variable Overhead
All manufacturing costs including fixed overhead
4-20.e: Throughput costing (super-variable costing) treats Only Direct Materials as product costs. Direct Labor and All overhead costs (both fixed and variable) are treated as period costs. This reflects Toc's view that only DM is truly variable in the short run.

4-20.q: Throughput Contribution is calculated as:
o: Sales Revenue - Direct Materials Cost only
Sales Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold
4-20.e: Throughput Contribution = Sales Revenue - Direct Materials Cost Only. Unlike contribution margin (which subtracts All variable costs), throughput contribution only subtracts direct materials — treating DL and OH as fixed period costs.

4-20.q: Product A: Sales $120, DM $60, bottleneck hours 2. Product B: Sales $90, DM $30, bottleneck hours 1. Which should be prioritised?
o: Product A — higher total throughput contribution
Product B — higher throughput per bottleneck hour
4-20.e: Product A throughput/hour = ($120-$60)/2 = $30/hr. Product B throughput/hour = ($90-$30)/1 = $60/hr. Prioritise Product B because it generates more throughput per scarce bottleneck hour — even though Product A has a higher total throughput contribution ($60 vs $60).

4-20.q: After successfully elevating a bottleneck, a Toc manager should:
o: Stop improvement efforts — the constraint is resolved
Return to Step 1 and identify the New constraint
Focus on reducing costs in all departments equally
Implement standard costing across the system
4-20.e: Toc Step 5 is Repeat — once you elevate the current bottleneck, a New constraint will emerge elsewhere in the system. The manager must return to Step 1 and identify the new bottleneck. Continuous constraint management is the Toc philosophy.

4-20.q: Process Time 30 minutes. Lead Time 300 minutes. Value-Added Ratio =
4-20.e: Value-Added Ratio = Process Time / Lead Time = 30 / 300 = 10%. Only 10% of total lead time is spent adding value. The remaining 90% is non-value-added (waiting, transport, inspection, etc.) — a major lean improvement opportunity.

4-21.q: It has been shown that a company's commitment to quality can lead to higher levels of productivity. All of the following are reasons why productivity increases result from a commitment to quality except
o: Customer satisfaction is improved because customers know the quality of the product they are buying
It instills a quality consciousness within employees where they care about doing it right the first time
Producing fewer defects means fewer resources are being wasted
It identifies non-value-adding activities, thereby making processes more efficient
4-21.e: Improved customer satisfaction will likely be a result of a commitment to quality. However, it is not a reason why productivity increases result from a commitment to quality. As a company’s commitment to quality increases, usually productivity also increases. Reasons for this increase in productivity include (1) a reduction in the number of defective units, leading to fewer resources wasted; (2) a more efficient manufacturing process, leading to removal or change of unproductive or non-value-adding activities; and (3) a commitment on the part of employees to doing things right the first time, which leads to a reduction in the number of defective units and fewer wasted resources.
wrongWhy: As a company’s commitment to quality increases, usually productivity also increases. Reasons why productivity increases result from a commitment to quality include a commitment on the part of employees to doing things right the first time, which leads to a reduction in the number of defective units and fewer wasted resources. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

As a company’s commitment to quality increases, usually productivity also increases. Reasons why productivity increases result from a commitment to quality include a reduction in the number of defective units, leading to fewer resources wasted. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

As a company’s commitment to quality increases, usually productivity also increases. Reasons why productivity increases result from a commitment to quality include a more efficient manufacturing process, leading to removal or change of unproductive or non-value-adding activities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 2.

4-21.q: In the cost of quality, downtime caused by quality problems is an example of a(n):
4-21.e: Internal failure costs are incurred when low quality products are produced, but detected before shipment to the customer. The downtime cost due to a quality problem connected to a defective product produced is an example of an internal failure cost.
wrongWhy: Prevention costs are costs incurred prevent the production of defective products. Downtime costs due to a quality problem from a defective product already produced is not a prevention cost.

Appraisal costs include the cost of any activity designed to test, appraise, or check for defective products. Downtime costs due to a quality problem from a defective product already produced is not an appraisal cost.

The costs involved when a defective product has already reached the customer are external failure costs. Downtime costs due to a quality problem from a defective product already produced is not an external failure cost. 3.

4-21.q: External failure costs include all of the following costs except those related to
4-21.e: External failure occurs when a defect is not detected until the product is with the consumer. Product field testing is a part of product design, so it takes place before full production begins and certainly before shipment of the finished product to a customer. Therefore, it is not an external failure cost. It is, rather, a prevention cost.
wrongWhy: External failure occurs when a defect is not detected until the product is with the consumer. A product liability suit would arise as a result of a defective product that reaches a customer or customers, so it is an external failure cost.

External failure occurs when a defect is not detected until the product is with the consumer. Lost sales and lost customers are a cost of providing consumers with an inferior product, so lost sales and lost customers are an external failure cost.

External failure occurs when a defect is not detected until the product is with the consumer. The cost of fulfilling warranty obligations is a cost of providing the customer with an inferior product, so warranty obligations are an external failure cost. 4.

4-21.q: In measuring the cost of quality, which one of the following is considered an appraisal cost?
4-21.e: Appraisal costs are the costs that are incurred in order to determine if an individual unit is defective. Product testing is done to find units that do not meet specifications, so the cost of product testing is an appraisal cost.
wrongWhy: Appraisal costs are the costs that are incurred in order to determine if an individual unit is defective. The cost of warranty claims is an external failure cost. External failure costs are costs to correct a defect that is not discovered until the product is in the consumer's hands.
Equipment maintenance cost is a prevention cost. Prevention costs are costs that are incurred in order to prevent a defect from occurring.
Rework cost is an internal failure cost. Internal failure occurs when a problem is detected before shipment to the customer. 5.

4-21.q: The use of teams in total quality management is important because:
o: Teams are quicker to make decisions, thereby helping to reduce cycle time
The use of teams eliminates the need for supervision, thereby allowing a company to become leaner and more profitable
Well-managed teams can be highly creative and are able to address complex problems better than individuals can
Employee motivation is higher for team members than for individual contributors
4-21.e: Teams can use the diverse knowledge and skills of all team members.
wrongWhy: Teams are often inefficient and costly.
Although need for supervision may be reduced, it is not eliminated.
Although employee motivation may be high in teams, the high motivation does not always translate directly to quality improvement. 6.

4-21.q: Total quality management (TQM) is built on a philosophy based on several beliefs. Which of the following is not part of TQM's four major objectives:
o: Enhanced and consistent quality of the product or service
Elimination of non-value adding work or processes, which leads to lower costs
Timely and consistent responses to customer needs
A stable and unchanging product or service
4-21.e: Rather than a having stable and unchanging product or service, an objective of TQM is quick adaptation and flexibility in response to the shifting requirements of customers.
wrongWhy: Enhanced and consistent quality of the product or service is one of the four major objectives of TQM.
Elimination of non-value adding work or processes, which leads to lower costs, is a major objective of TQM.
Timely and consistent responses to customer needs is a major objective of TQM. 7.

4-21.q: Total quality management (TQM) stresses that all of the organization's operations must be oriented toward improving the reliability of which of the following:
4-21.e: TQM is an approach that is committed to customer satisfaction and continuous improvement of the company's products or services.
wrongWhy: Compensation methods may contribute only to employee attitudes toward TQM.

Internal and external analysis is concerned with identifying the strengths and weakness of the company to provides managers with the information they need to build the strategies and business models necessary to attaining and sustaining competitive advantage, but this does not revolve around TQM.
Employee practices may contribute only to employee attitudes toward TQM. 8.

4-21.q: Which of the following is not an objective of a company’s TQM program?
o: To evaluate environmental issues that may affect the company’s profitability
To examine issues relating to creating value for the company’s customers
To understand the company’s capacity for innovation
To utilize computers in product development, analysis, and design modifications
4-21.e: TQM focuses on enhancing the quality of the product or service with consistency, providing a high level of customer service, eliminating non-value-adding work, and responding quickly and flexibly to the shifting requirements of customers. Computer-aided design (CAD) utilizes computers in the product development, analysis and design modification stages, so CAD may be used in implementing TQM objectives; but CAD itself is not an objective of TQM.
wrongWhy: This is an objective of a company’s TQM program. Total Quality Management describes a management approach that is committed to customer satisfaction and continuous improvement of products or services. A premise of TQM is that a company should always strive to perform its service or produce its product correctly the first time, because that is a way to increase revenues and decrease costs. For a manufacturer, that means manufacturing quality products within the required specifications. Manufacturing quality products within the required specifications includes adhering to all environmental protection rules and regulations during the manufacturing process, because a violation could result in penalties and fines, thus negatively impacting the company's profitability. Therefore, evaluating environmental issues and the company's compliance with environmental protection requirements is an objective of a company's TQM program.

This is an objective of a company’s TQM program. Total Quality Management describes a management approach that is committed to customer satisfaction and continuous improvement of products or services. A premise of TQM is that a company should always strive to perform its service or produce its product correctly the first time, because that is a way to increase revenues and decrease costs. Objectives of TQM include elimination of non-value-adding work or processes, which leads to lower costs. Therefore, examining issues relating to creating value for the company's customers is an objective of a company's TQM program.

This is an objective of a company’s TQM program. Total Quality Management describes a management approach that is committed to customer satisfaction and continuous improvement of products or services. Customer satisfaction and continuous improvement of products and services requires innovative thinking in order to find ways to continually improve. Therefore, understanding the company's capacity for innovation is an objective of a company's TQM program. 9.

4-21.q: In the cost of quality, which of the following is an example of a prevention cost?
o: Cost of replacing parts returned by customers
Cost incurred by reworking on defective parts detected by the quality assurance group
Cost of inspecting products by quality inspectors during the production process
Labor cost of product designers whose task is to design components that will withstand extreme temperature conditions
4-21.e: Product designers should ensure that the design of the product is such that it will not break under extreme temperature conditions. This is a prevention cost. This cost is incurred before the damage has occurred and thereby prevents failure from occurring.
wrongWhy: This is an example of external failure cost.
This is an example of an internal failure cost.
This is an example of an appraisal cost. 10.

4-21.q: When measuring the cost of quality, the cost of inspecting all incoming raw materials is a(n)
4-21.e: Appraisal costs are the costs that are incurred in order to determine if an individual unit is defective. Inspection of all incoming raw materials is done in order to determine if any of the raw materials are defective, so it is an appraisal cost.
wrongWhy: External failure occurs a defect is not detected until the product is already with the consumer. Inspection of incoming raw materials takes place before production.

Prevention costs are the costs that are incurred in order to prevent a defect from occurring. Evaluation and testing of raw materials received from a new supplier or an ongoing program to evaluate the quality of existing suppliers' materials to confirm their conformance to the company’s standards is a prevention cost because it is done to prevent the receipt of defective raw materials in the future. If too many defects are found in materials from a new or an existing supplier, that supplier will not be used in the future, and that will prevent receipt of defective raw materials in the future (at least from that supplier). However, a procedure to check all incoming raw materials as they are received that is done in order to determine whether an individual unit received is defective is an appraisal cost.
Internal failure occurs when a problem is detected after production but before shipment to the customer. Inspection of incoming raw materials takes place before production. 11.

4-21.q: Of these statements, which is not relevant to the overall benchmarking process?
o: Management uses target costing to set standard costs in order to put the focus on the market as well as on what target price to charge because the company must attain the target cost to realize its desired profit margin for the product
Target costing utilizes kaizen to reduce costs in order to attain a desired profit margin
Management puts into place a program to measure the organization against the products, practices and services of the most efficient competitors in its marketplace
Management determines what processes the company uses to convert materials, capital and labor into value-adding products
4-21.e: Determining what processes the company uses to convert materials, capital and labor into value-adding products is not part of benchmarking. Rather, benchmarking involves measuring the company’s products, practices and services against successful competitors, using the competitors’ standards as goals for its own operations. This “best practices” process helps the company to cut costs, boost output and achieve competitive advantage. Companies use target costing to set standard costs that will create the desired gross profit margin for their products sold at the target price. Target costing utilizes kaizen, or continuous improvement, to reduce costs and attain desired gross profit margins.
wrongWhy: This is relevant to the overall benchmarking process.
This is relevant to the overall benchmarking process.
This is relevant to the overall benchmarking process. 12.

4-21.q: Curtis Company is currently performing a cost of quality analysis of its facilities. The following costs were compiled by the factory accountant: What is the total internal failure cost and what is the total external failure cost?
o: Total internal failure costs: $531; Total external failure costs: $1,873
Total internal failure costs: $2,205; Total external failure costs: $7,803
Total internal failure costs: $1,873; Total external failure costs: $7,803
Total internal failure costs: $1,873; Total external failure costs: $1,683
4-21.e: Internal failure costs are: spoilage, scrap and rework ($581 + $135 + $1,157 = $1,873) and the external failure costs are: warranty repair, field repairs and liability claims ($2,520 + $3600 + $1,683 = $7,803).
wrongWhy: $531 is a prevention cost and $1,873 is the total internal failure cost.
$2,205 is the total appraisal cost (inspection and product testing ($1,350 + $855)), which is not a cost of nonconformance. The total external failure costs amount is correct.

$1,873 is the total internal failure cost, which is spoilage, scrap and rework ($581 + $135 + $1,157 = $1,873), but $1,683 is the liability claims, which is only one of the external failure costs. 13.

4-21.q: For most companies the quality of its products is a key factor when measuring performance. Which of the following would probably not be used as a product quality performance measurement?
o: The time spent on reworking defective products
4-21.e: Idle time is a manufacturing efficiency measure. It is not used to measure quality.
wrongWhy: Time spent on reworking defective products is an important measurement of quality in manufacturing. The more defective products are produced, the more time will be spent in reworking them, and vice versa. To limit rework time, limit defective proucts.

The number of sales returns can be an important measurement of quality, because defective goods may be returned for a refund, if the customer does not wish to be bothered making a claim under a warranty, if a warranty is available.
The number of customer complaints is an important indicator of the quality of a company's products. 14.

4-21.q: Listed below are selected line items from the cost-of-quality report for Watson Products for last month. Category Amount Rework $ 725 What is Watson's total prevention and appraisal cost for last month?
4-21.e: Total prevention and appraisal cost should include equipment maintenance and product testing costs. Thus, the total cost incurred is $1,940.
wrongWhy: The cost of $2,665 includes rework, which is an internal failure cost and should not be included.
The cost of $1,154 is for equipment maintenance, which is a prevention cost. Also, needs to include product testing, which is an appraisal cost.
The cost of $786 is for product testing, which is an appraisal cost. Also, needs to include equipment maintenance, which is a prevention cost. 15.

4-21.q: The cost of scrap, rework, and tooling changes in a product quality cost system is categorized as a(n)
4-21.e: The cost of scrap, rework and tooling changes is categorized as an internal failure cost. Internal failure occurs when a problem is detected before the product is shipped to any customer. Internal failure costs include rework, scrap, tooling changes, downtime required to do the tooling changes, and costs associated with rushing to complete an order because of failure to do the work properly the first time.
wrongWhy: The cost of scrap, rework and tooling changes is not categorized as an external failure cost.
The cost of scrap, rework and tooling changes is not categorized as an appraisal cost.
The cost of scrap, rework and tooling changes is not categorized as a prevention cost. 16.

4-21.q: To achieve total quality management (TQM), the company must identify the relevant quality problems when and/or where they occur, and then use methods to analyze them. Which of the following is not one of these methods?
4-21.e: Drum-Buffer-Rope is a means of applying Theory of Constraints principles to manufacturing. It is not applicable to TQM.
wrongWhy: A Pareto diagram is used in analyzing qualify problems in a TQM environment. A Pareto diagram is a specific type of histogram. Utilizing the Pareto 80-20 principle (20% of the population causes 80% of the problems), it helps management to pinpoint which 20% of the causes are accounting for 80% of the problems. In this way, it helps management to focus efforts on improving the areas that are likely to have the greatest overall positive impact.

A control chart is a method used in analyzing quality problems in a TQM environment. A control chart records observations of an operation taken at regular intervals. If there are trends, clusters, or many measurements outside or nearly outside the specified range, the process may be out of control.

A histogram is a method for analyzing quality problems in a TQM environment. A histogram is a bar graph that shows the frequency of events in a set of data. Patterns that may not be apparent when just looking at a set of numbers become clear in a histogram, and problem areas can be pinpointed. For instance, if a particular production line is experiencing most of the difficulty, a histogram can help determine what types of problems are occurring most frequently. 17.

4-21.q: A company is currently performing a cost of quality analysis of one of its facilities. The following are costs compiled by the facility accountant. Total internal failure cost is
4-21.e: Internal failure occurs when the company detects a problem before shipment to the customer occurs. The costs listed that are internal failure costs are Spoilage $ 645 Scrap 150 Rework 1,285 Total internal failure costs $2,080
